title: City

infobox.value: is a [human settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") of a substantial size. The term "city" has different meanings around the world and in some places the settlement can be very small. Even where the term is limited to larger settlements, there is no universally agreed definition of the lower boundary for their size.[[1]](#cite_note-Goodall-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) In a narrower sense, a city can be defined as a permanent and [densely](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density") populated place with administratively defined boundaries whose members work primarily on non-agricultural tasks.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Cities generally have extensive systems for [housing](/wiki/Housing "Housing"), [transportation](/wiki/Transport "Transport"), [sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation"), [utilities](/wiki/Public_utilities "Public utilities"), [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use"), [production of goods](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), and [communication](/wiki/Communication "Communication"). Their density facilitates interaction between people, [government organizations](/wiki/Government_organisations "Government organisations"), and [businesses](/wiki/Business "Business"), sometimes benefiting different parties in the process, such as improving the efficiency of goods and service distribution.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Large permanent human settlement

For other uses, see [City (disambiguation)](/wiki/City_(disambiguation) "City (disambiguation)").

From top, left to right: [Westminster Palace](/wiki/Westminster_Palace "Westminster Palace") in London; [Shibuya Crossing](/wiki/Shibuya_Crossing "Shibuya Crossing") in Tokyo; [painting of Boulevard Montmartre](/wiki/Le_Boulevard_de_Montmartre,_Matin%C3%A9e_de_Printemps "Le Boulevard de Montmartre, Matinée de Printemps") in Paris by [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"); the [Rocinha](/wiki/Rocinha "Rocinha") [favela](/wiki/Favela "Favela") in Rio de Janeiro; [6th Avenue in Manhattan](/wiki/6th_Avenue_(Manhattan) "6th Avenue (Manhattan)"); spice market in [Old Delhi](/wiki/Old_Delhi "Old Delhi"); [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"); and the [São Paulo Metro](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Metro "São Paulo Metro")

A **city** is a [human settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") of a substantial size. The term "city" has different meanings around the world and in some places the settlement can be very small. Even where the term is limited to larger settlements, there is no universally agreed definition of the lower boundary for their size.[[1]](#cite_note-Goodall-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) In a narrower sense, a city can be defined as a permanent and [densely](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density") populated place with administratively defined boundaries whose members work primarily on non-agricultural tasks.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Cities generally have extensive systems for [housing](/wiki/Housing "Housing"), [transportation](/wiki/Transport "Transport"), [sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation"), [utilities](/wiki/Public_utilities "Public utilities"), [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use"), [production of goods](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), and [communication](/wiki/Communication "Communication"). Their density facilitates interaction between people, [government organizations](/wiki/Government_organisations "Government organisations"), and [businesses](/wiki/Business "Business"), sometimes benefiting different parties in the process, such as improving the efficiency of goods and service distribution.

Historically, city dwellers have been a small proportion of humanity overall, but following two centuries of unprecedented and rapid [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), more than half of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population") now lives in cities, which has had profound consequences for [global sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Present-day cities usually form the core of larger [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") and [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area")—creating numerous [commuters](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") traveling toward [city centres](/wiki/City_centre "City centre") for employment, entertainment, and education. However, in a world of intensifying [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization"), all cities are to varying degrees also connected globally beyond these regions. This increased influence means that cities also have significant influences on [global issues](/wiki/Global_issues "Global issues"), such as [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"), [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_and_cities "Climate change and cities"), and [global health](/wiki/Global_health "Global health"). Because of these major influences on global issues, the [international community](/wiki/International_community "International community") has prioritized investment in [sustainable cities](/wiki/Sustainable_city "Sustainable city") through [Sustainable Development Goal 11](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goal_11 "Sustainable Development Goal 11"). Due to the efficiency of transportation and the smaller [land consumption](/wiki/Land_consumption "Land consumption"), [dense](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density") cities hold the potential to have a smaller [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") per inhabitant than more sparsely populated areas.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Therefore, [compact cities](/wiki/Compact_city "Compact city") are often referred to as a crucial element in fighting climate change.[[7]](#cite_note-7) However, this concentration can also have some significant harmful effects, such as forming [urban heat islands](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island"), concentrating [pollution](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution"), and stressing water supplies and other resources.

## Meaning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Meaning")]

### Urban settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Urban settlements")]

Common population definitions for an *urban area* (city *or* town) range between 1,500 and 50,000 people, with most [U.S.](/wiki/U.S. "U.S.") states using a minimum between 1,500 and 5,000 inhabitants.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Hugo-9) Some jurisdictions set no such criteria.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

National [censuses](/wiki/Census "Census") use a variety of definitions – invoking factors such as [population](/wiki/Population "Population"), [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density"), number of [dwellings](/wiki/Dwelling "Dwelling"), economic function, and [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") – to classify populations as urban.[[11]](#cite_note-reg-11)

### City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: City")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Sheth_Motisha_Tonk_01.jpg/250px-Sheth_Motisha_Tonk_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheth_Motisha_Tonk_01.jpg)

[Palitana](/wiki/Palitana "Palitana") is a religiously significant city in Gujarat, India.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

A *city* can be distinguished from other human settlements by its relatively great size, but also by its functions and its [special symbolic status](/wiki/City_status "City status"), which may be conferred by a central authority. The term can also refer either to the physical streets and buildings of the city or to the collection of people who dwell there and can be used in a general sense to mean [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") rather than [rural territory](/wiki/Rural_territory "Rural territory").[[13]](#cite_note-OED-13)[[14]](#cite_note-KAL-14)

According to the "functional definition", a city is not distinguished by size alone, but also by the role it plays within a larger political context. Cities serve as administrative, commercial, religious, and cultural hubs for their larger surrounding areas.[[15]](#cite_note-Smith-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The presence of a literate elite is often associated with cities because of the cultural diversities present in a city.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) A typical city has professional [administrators](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration"), regulations, and some form of [taxation](/wiki/Taxation "Taxation") (food and other necessities or means to trade for them) to support the [government workers](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service"). (This arrangement contrasts with the more typically [horizontal](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") relationships in a [tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") or [village](/wiki/Village "Village") accomplishing common goals through informal agreements between neighbors, or the [leadership](/wiki/Leadership "Leadership") of a chief.) The governments may be based on heredity, religion, military power, work systems such as canal-building, food distribution, land-ownership, agriculture, commerce, manufacturing, finance, or a combination of these. Societies that live in cities are often called [civilizations](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization").

#### Population size, density

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Population size, density")]

The *degree of urbanization* is a modern metric to help define what comprises a city: "a population of at least 50,000 inhabitants in contiguous dense grid cells (>1,500 inhabitants per square kilometer)".[[19]](#cite_note-19) This metric was "devised over years by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and others, and endorsed in March [2021] by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") ... largely for the purpose of international statistical comparison".[[20]](#cite_note-20) Typical working definitions for small-city populations start at around 100,000 people.[[11]](#cite_note-reg-11)

#### In specific countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In specific countries")]

[In Australia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia "List of cities in Australia"), the definition of what constitutes a city varies between the states.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [United Kingdom, city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") is awarded by the Crown and then remains permanent, with only two exceptions to this rule due to policy changes. A lack of official qualifying criteria results in some particularly small cities, notably [St Davids](/wiki/St_Davids "St Davids") with a population of 1,751 as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit).

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *city* comes from the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French_language "Old French language"): *cité*, itself from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *civitas*, meaning "citizenship" or "state".[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The related (maybe) *[civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization")* come from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") root *[civitas](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/civitas "wikt:civitas")*, originally meaning 'citizenship' or 'community member' and eventually coming to correspond with *[urbs](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/urbs "wikt:urbs")*, meaning 'city' in a more physical sense.[[13]](#cite_note-OED-13) The Roman *civitas* was closely linked with the Greek *[polis](/wiki/Polis "Polis")*—another common root appearing in English words such as *[metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis "Metropolis")*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In [toponymic](/wiki/Toponymic "Toponymic") terminology, names of individual cities and towns are called *astionyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἄστυ 'city or town' and ὄνομα 'name').[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199613-24)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[Urban geography](/wiki/Urban_geography "Urban geography") deals both with cities in their larger context and with their internal structure.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Cities are estimated to cover about 3% of the land surface of the Earth.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Site")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Allegheny_Monongahela_Ohio.jpg/250px-Allegheny_Monongahela_Ohio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allegheny_Monongahela_Ohio.jpg)

[Downtown Pittsburgh](/wiki/Downtown_Pittsburgh "Downtown Pittsburgh") at the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the [Monongahela](/wiki/Monongahela_River "Monongahela River") and [Allegheny](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") rivers, which flow into the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River")

Town siting has varied through history according to natural, technological, economic, and military contexts. Access to water has long been a major factor in city placement and growth, and despite exceptions enabled by the advent of [rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") in the nineteenth century, through the present most of the world's urban population lives near the coast or on a river.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Urban areas as a rule cannot [produce their own food](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") and therefore must develop some [relationship](/wiki/City_region "City region") with a [hinterland](/wiki/Hinterland "Hinterland") that sustains them.[[28]](#cite_note-Kaplan155-28) Only in special cases such as [mining towns](/wiki/Mining_town "Mining town") which play a vital role in long-distance trade, are cities disconnected from the countryside which feeds them.[[29]](#cite_note-Marshall-29) Thus, centrality within a productive region influences siting, as economic forces would, in theory, favor the creation of marketplaces in optimal mutually reachable locations.[[30]](#cite_note-Latham-30)

### Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Center")]

Main article: [City centre](/wiki/City_centre "City centre")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Helsinginkeskustailmakuva_04.JPG/250px-Helsinginkeskustailmakuva_04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Helsinginkeskustailmakuva_04.JPG)

[Kluuvi](/wiki/Kluuvi "Kluuvi"), a city centre in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland

The vast majority of cities have a central area containing buildings with special economic, political, and religious significance. Archaeologists refer to this area by the Greek term [temenos](/wiki/Temenos "Temenos") or if fortified as a [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel").[[31]](#cite_note-31) These spaces historically reflect and amplify the city's centrality and importance to its wider [sphere of influence](/wiki/City_region "City region").[[30]](#cite_note-Latham-30) Today cities have a [city center](/wiki/City_center "City center") or [downtown](/wiki/Downtown "Downtown"), sometimes coincident with a [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district").

### Public space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Public space")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Trafalgar_Square%2C_London_2_-_Jun_2009.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square%2C_London_2_-_Jun_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square,_London_2_-_Jun_2009.jpg)

[Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square"), a public meeting place in central [London](/wiki/London "London")

Cities typically have [public spaces](/wiki/Public_space "Public space") where anyone can go. These include [privately owned spaces open to the public](/wiki/Privately_owned_public_space "Privately owned public space") as well as forms of public land such as [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain_(land) "Public domain (land)") and the [commons](/wiki/Common_land "Common land"). [Western philosophy](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy") since the time of the Greek [agora](/wiki/Agora "Agora") has considered physical public space as the substrate of the symbolic [public sphere](/wiki/Public_sphere "Public sphere").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009177–179-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) [Public art](/wiki/Public_art "Public art") adorns (or disfigures) public spaces. [Parks](/wiki/Park "Park") and other [natural sites within cities](/wiki/Incorporation_of_nature_within_a_city "Incorporation of nature within a city") provide residents with relief from the hardness and regularity of typical [built environments](/wiki/Built_environment "Built environment"). [Urban green spaces](/wiki/Urban_green_spaces "Urban green spaces") are another component of public space that provides the benefit of mitigating the urban heat island effect, especially in cities that are in warmer climates. These spaces prevent carbon imbalances, extreme habitat losses, electricity and water consumption, and human health risks.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Internal structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Internal structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/L%27Enfant_plan.svg/250px-L%27Enfant_plan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:L%27Enfant_plan.svg)

The [L'Enfant Plan](/wiki/L%27Enfant_Plan "L'Enfant Plan") for [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") combines a utilitarian grid pattern with diagonal avenues and a symbolic focus on monumental architecture.

[The urban structure](/wiki/Urban_structure "Urban structure") generally follows one or more basic patterns: geomorphic, radial, concentric, rectilinear, and curvilinear. The physical environment generally constrains the form in which a city is built. If located on a mountainside, urban structures may rely on terraces and winding roads. It may be adapted to its means of subsistence (e.g. agriculture or fishing). And it may be set up for optimal defense given the surrounding landscape.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Beyond these "geomorphic" features, cities can develop internal patterns, due to natural growth or to [city planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning").

In a radial structure, main roads converge on a central point. This form could evolve from successive growth over a long time, with concentric traces of [town walls](/wiki/Town_wall "Town wall") and [citadels](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel") marking older city boundaries. In more recent history, such forms were supplemented by [ring roads](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road") moving traffic around the outskirts of a town. Dutch cities such as [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and [Haarlem](/wiki/Haarlem "Haarlem") are structured as a central square surrounded by concentric canals marking every expansion. In cities such as [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), this pattern is still clearly visible.

A system of rectilinear city streets and land plots, known as the [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), has been used for millennia in Asia, Europe, and the Americas. The [Indus Valley Civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") built [Mohenjo-Daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-Daro "Mohenjo-Daro"), [Harappa](/wiki/Harappa "Harappa"), and other cities on a grid pattern, using ancient principles described by [Kautilya](/wiki/Kautilya "Kautilya"), and aligned with the [compass points](/wiki/Compass_points "Compass points").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[15]](#cite_note-Smith-15)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) The ancient Greek city of [Priene](/wiki/Priene "Priene") exemplifies a grid plan with specialized districts used across the [Hellenistic Mediterranean](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period").

### Urban areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Urban areas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/National_Bank_of_Egypt-2020.jpg/250px-National_Bank_of_Egypt-2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Bank_of_Egypt-2020.jpg)

The [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") banks in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo")

The urban-type settlement extends far beyond the traditional boundaries of the [city proper](/wiki/City_proper "City proper")[[39]](#cite_note-39) in a form of development sometimes described critically as [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl").[[40]](#cite_note-40) Decentralization and dispersal of city functions (commercial, industrial, residential, cultural, political) has transformed the very meaning of the term and has challenged geographers seeking to classify territories according to an urban-rural binary.[[9]](#cite_note-Hugo-9)

[Metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") include [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") and [exurbs](/wiki/Exurb "Exurb") organized around the needs of [commuters](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting"), and sometimes [edge cities](/wiki/Edge_city "Edge city") characterized by a degree of economic and political independence. (In the US these are grouped into [metropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_areas "Metropolitan statistical areas") for purposes of [demography](/wiki/Demography "Demography") and [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing").) Some cities are now part of a continuous urban landscape called [urban agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration"), [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"), or [megalopolis](/wiki/Megalopolis "Megalopolis") (exemplified by the [BosWash](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") corridor of the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States").)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of cities](/wiki/History_of_cities "History of cities")

Further information: [Urban history](/wiki/Urban_history "Urban history"), [Historical urban community sizes](/wiki/Historical_urban_community_sizes "Historical urban community sizes"), and [List of largest cities throughout history](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_throughout_history "List of largest cities throughout history")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Oldest_arch_4.JPG/250px-Oldest_arch_4.JPG)](/wiki/File:Oldest_arch_4.JPG)

An [arch](/wiki/Arch "Arch") from the ancient [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") city [Ur](/wiki/Ur "Ur"), which flourished in the [third millennium BC](/wiki/Third_millennium_BC "Third millennium BC"), can be seen at present-day Tell el-Mukayyar in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Mohenjo-daro.jpg/250px-Mohenjo-daro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mohenjo-daro.jpg)

[Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro"), a city of the [Indus Valley Civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilization "Indus Valley Civilization") in Pakistan, which was rebuilt six or more times, using bricks of standard size, and adhering to the same grid layout—also in the third millennium BC

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Teotihuac%C3%A1n_2012-09-28_00-07-11.jpg/250px-Teotihuac%C3%A1n_2012-09-28_00-07-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teotihuac%C3%A1n_2012-09-28_00-07-11.jpg)

Aerial view of what was once downtown [Teotihuacan](/wiki/Teotihuacan "Teotihuacan") showing the [Pyramid of the Sun](/wiki/Pyramid_of_the_Sun "Pyramid of the Sun"), [Pyramid of the Moon](/wiki/Pyramid_of_the_Moon "Pyramid of the Moon"), and the processional avenue serving as the spine of the city's street system

The emergence of cities from [proto-urban settlements](/wiki/Proto-city "Proto-city"), such as [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), is a non-linear development that demonstrates the varied experiences of early [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization").[[42]](#cite_note-42) The cities of [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho"), [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"), [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos"), [Faiyum](/wiki/Faiyum "Faiyum"), [Yerevan](/wiki/Yerevan "Yerevan"), [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), [Matera](/wiki/Matera "Matera"), [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus"), and [Argos](/wiki/Argos,_Peloponnese "Argos, Peloponnese") are among those laying claim to [the longest continual inhabitation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_continuously_inhabited_cities "List of oldest continuously inhabited cities").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Cities, characterized by [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density"), [symbolic](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol") function, and [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), have existed for thousands of years.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In the conventional view, civilization and the city were both followed by the [development of agriculture](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"), which enabled the production of surplus food and thus a social [division of labor](/wiki/Division_of_labour "Division of labour") (with concomitant [social stratification](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification")) and [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade").[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Early cities often featured [granaries](/wiki/Granary "Granary"), sometimes within a temple.[[48]](#cite_note-48) A minority viewpoint considers that cities may have arisen without agriculture, due to alternative means of subsistence (fishing),[[49]](#cite_note-49) to use as communal seasonal shelters,[[50]](#cite_note-Perlman-50) to their value as bases for defensive and offensive military organization,[[51]](#cite_note-Mumfurd1961war-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Ashworth1991p12-52) or to their inherent economic function.[[53]](#cite_note-Jacobs_1969_23-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Cities played a crucial role in the establishment of political power over an area, and ancient leaders such as [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") founded and created them with zeal.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Ancient times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ancient times")]

Further information: [Cities of the Ancient Near East](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Ancient_Near_East "Cities of the Ancient Near East"), [Polis](/wiki/Polis "Polis"), [City-state](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), and [Late Antiquity § Cities](/wiki/Late_Antiquity#Cities "Late Antiquity")

"Ancient city" redirects here. For other uses, see [Ancient city (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ancient_city_(disambiguation) "Ancient city (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Artgate_Fondazione_Cariplo_-_Betti_Oreste_-_Veduta_della_Roma_imperiale.jpg/250px-Artgate_Fondazione_Cariplo_-_Betti_Oreste_-_Veduta_della_Roma_imperiale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artgate_Fondazione_Cariplo_-_Betti_Oreste_-_Veduta_della_Roma_imperiale.jpg)

A modern depiction of [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), the first city in the world to reach one million inhabitants

[Jericho](/wiki/Tell_es-Sultan "Tell es-Sultan") and [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), dated to the [eighth millennium BC](/wiki/Eighth_millennium_BC "Eighth millennium BC"), are among the earliest [proto-cities](/wiki/Proto-cities "Proto-cities") known to archaeologists.[[50]](#cite_note-Perlman-50)[[57]](#cite_note-57) However, the [Mesopotamian](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") city of [Uruk](/wiki/Uruk "Uruk") from the mid-fourth millennium BC (ancient Iraq) is considered by most archaeologists to be the first true city, innovating many characteristics for cities to follow, with its name attributed to the [Uruk period](/wiki/Uruk_period "Uruk period").[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In the [fourth](/wiki/Fourth_millennium_BC "Fourth millennium BC") and [third millennium BC](/wiki/Third_millennium_BC "Third millennium BC"), complex civilizations flourished in the river valleys of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [India](/wiki/India "India"),[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) [China](/wiki/China "China"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). Excavations in these areas have found the [ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins") of cities geared variously towards trade, politics, or religion. Some had large, [dense populations](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density"), but others carried out urban activities in the realms of politics or religion without having large associated populations.

Among the early Old World cities, [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro") of the Indus Valley civilization in present-day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), existing from about 2600 BC, was one of the largest, with a population of 50,000 or more and a [sophisticated sanitation system](/wiki/Sanitation_of_the_Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Sanitation of the Indus Valley Civilisation").[[64]](#cite_note-64) [China's planned cities](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_urban_planning "Ancient Chinese urban planning") were constructed according to sacred principles to act as celestial [microcosms](/wiki/Macrocosm_and_microcosm "Macrocosm and microcosm").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The [Ancient Egyptian cities](/wiki/List_of_ancient_Egyptian_towns_and_cities "List of ancient Egyptian towns and cities") known physically by archaeologists are not extensive.[[15]](#cite_note-Smith-15) They include (known by their Arab names) [El Lahun](/wiki/El_Lahun "El Lahun"), a workers' town associated with the pyramid of [Senusret II](/wiki/Senusret_II "Senusret II"), and the religious city [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna") built by [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten") and abandoned. These sites appear planned in a highly regimented and [stratified](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification") fashion, with a minimalistic grid of rooms for the workers and increasingly more elaborate housing available for higher classes.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In Mesopotamia, the civilization of [Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), followed by [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria") and [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), gave rise to numerous cities, governed by kings and fostered multiple languages written in [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform").[[67]](#cite_note-67) The [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") trading empire, flourishing around the turn of the [first millennium BC](/wiki/First_millennium_BC "First millennium BC"), encompassed [numerous cities](/wiki/List_of_Phoenician_cities "List of Phoenician cities") extending from [Tyre](/wiki/Tyre,_Lebanon "Tyre, Lebanon"), [Cydon](/wiki/Cydon "Cydon"), and [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos") to [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") and [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz").

In the following centuries, independent [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") of [Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), especially [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens"), developed the *[polis](/wiki/Polis "Polis")*, an association of male landowning [citizens](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship") who collectively constituted the city.[[68]](#cite_note-tws2Y21-68) The [agora](/wiki/Agora "Agora"), meaning "gathering place" or "assembly", was the center of the athletic, artistic, spiritual, and political life of the polis.[[69]](#cite_note-InternationalDictionary-69) [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") was the first city that surpassed one million inhabitants. Under the authority of [its empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), Rome transformed and [founded](/wiki/List_of_cities_founded_by_the_Romans "List of cities founded by the Romans") many cities (*[Colonia](/wiki/Colonia_(Roman) "Colonia (Roman)")*), and with them brought its principles of urban architecture, design, and society.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In the ancient [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), early urban traditions developed in the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") and [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"). In the Andes, the first urban centers developed in the [Norte Chico civilization](/wiki/Norte_Chico_civilization "Norte Chico civilization"), [Chavin](/wiki/Chav%C3%ADn_culture "Chavín culture") and [Moche](/wiki/Moche_(culture) "Moche (culture)") cultures, followed by major cities in the [Huari](/wiki/Huari_culture "Huari culture"), [Chimu](/wiki/Chimu "Chimu"), and [Inca](/wiki/Inca "Inca") cultures. The Norte Chico civilization included as many as 30 major population centers in what is now the [Norte Chico region](/wiki/Norte_Chico_(Peruvian_region) "Norte Chico (Peruvian region)") of north-central coastal [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"). It is the oldest known civilization in the Americas, flourishing between the 30th and 18th centuries BC.[[71]](#cite_note-Shady1997-71) Mesoamerica saw the rise of early urbanism in several cultural regions, beginning with the [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec") and spreading to the [Preclassic Maya](/wiki/Maya_city "Maya city"), the [Zapotec](/wiki/Zapotec_civilization "Zapotec civilization") of Oaxaca, and [Teotihuacan](/wiki/Teotihuacan "Teotihuacan") in central Mexico. Later cultures such as the [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec"), [Andean civilizations](/wiki/Andean_civilization "Andean civilization"), [Mayan](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples"), [Mississippians](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture"), and [Pueblo](/wiki/Pueblo "Pueblo") peoples drew on these earlier urban traditions. Many of their ancient cities continue to be inhabited, including major metropolitan cities such as [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), in the same location as [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan"); while ancient continuously inhabited Pueblos are near modern urban areas in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), such as [Acoma Pueblo](/wiki/Acoma_Pueblo "Acoma Pueblo") near the [Albuquerque metropolitan area](/wiki/Albuquerque_metropolitan_area "Albuquerque metropolitan area") and [Taos Pueblo](/wiki/Taos_Pueblo "Taos Pueblo") near [Taos](/wiki/Taos,_New_Mexico "Taos, New Mexico"); while others like [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima") are located nearby ancient [Peruvian](/wiki/Peru "Peru") sites such as [Pachacamac](/wiki/Pachacamac "Pachacamac").

From 1600 BC, [Dhar Tichitt](/wiki/Dhar_Tichitt "Dhar Tichitt"), in the south of present-day [Mauritania](/wiki/Mauritania "Mauritania"), presented characteristics suggestive of an incipient form of urbanism.[[72]](#cite_note-Monroe,_J._Cameron_2017-72)[[73]](#cite_note-MacDonald,_Kevin_2015-73) The second place to show urban characteristics in [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") was [Dia](/wiki/Dia,_Mali "Dia, Mali"), in present-day [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), from 800 BC.[[72]](#cite_note-Monroe,_J._Cameron_2017-72)[[73]](#cite_note-MacDonald,_Kevin_2015-73) Both Dhar Tichitt and Dia were founded by the same people: the [Soninke](/wiki/Soninke_people "Soninke people"), who would later also found the [Ghana Empire](/wiki/Ghana_Empire "Ghana Empire").[[73]](#cite_note-MacDonald,_Kevin_2015-73)

Another ancient site, [Jenné-Jeno](/wiki/Jenn%C3%A9-Jeno "Jenné-Jeno"), in what is today [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), has been dated to the third century BCE. According to Roderick and Susan McIntosh, Jenné-Jeno did not fit into traditional Western conceptions of urbanity as it lacked monumental architecture and a distinctive elite social class, but it should indeed be considered a city based on a functional redefinition of urban development. In particular, Jenné-Jeno featured settlement mounds arranged according to a horizontal, rather than vertical, power hierarchy, and served as a center of specialized production and exhibited functional interdependence with the surrounding hinterland.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

More recently, scholars have concluded that the [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") of Djenne-Djenno was likely established by the [Mande](/wiki/Mand%C3%A9_peoples "Mandé peoples") progenitors of the [Bozo people](/wiki/Bozo_people "Bozo people"). Their habitation of the site spanned the period from 3rd century BCE to 13th century CE.[[75]](#cite_note-Vydrin-75) Archaeological evidence from Jenné-Jeno, specifically the presence of non-West African glass beads dated from the third century BCE to the fourth century CE, indicates that pre-Arabic trade contacts probably existed between Jenné-Jeno and North Africa.[[76]](#cite_note-Magnavita-76)

Additionally, other early urban centers in West Africa, dated to around 500 CE, include [Awdaghust](/wiki/Aoudaghost "Aoudaghost"), [Kumbi Saleh](/wiki/Koumbi_Saleh "Koumbi Saleh"), the ancient capital of Ghana, and [Maranda](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marandet "de:Marandet"), a center located on a trade route between Egypt and Gao.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg/250px-Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg)

[Vyborg](/wiki/Vyborg "Vyborg") in Leningrad Oblast has existed since the 13th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Monumentale_torens_in_de_binnenstad.jpg/250px-Monumentale_torens_in_de_binnenstad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monumentale_torens_in_de_binnenstad.jpg)

Old city of [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht"), Netherlands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Holy_Roman_Empire_1648_Imperial_cities.png/250px-Holy_Roman_Empire_1648_Imperial_cities.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_Roman_Empire_1648_Imperial_cities.png)

The [Free imperial cities](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in 1648

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Haarlem-City-Map-1550.jpg/250px-Haarlem-City-Map-1550.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haarlem-City-Map-1550.jpg)

A map of [Haarlem](/wiki/Haarlem "Haarlem") in the Netherlands, created around 1550, shows the city completely surrounded by a [city wall](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall") and [defensive canal](/wiki/Moat "Moat"), with its square shape inspired by the shape of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").

The [dissolution of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") in the West was connected with profound changes in urban fabric of western Europe.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In places where Roman administration quickly weakened urbanism went through a profound crisis, even if it continued to remain an important symbolic factor.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In regions like Italy or Spain cities diminished in size but nevertheless continued to play a key role in both the economy and government.[[80]](#cite_note-80) [Late antique cities](/wiki/Late_antiquity#Cities "Late antiquity") in the East were also undergoing intense transformations, with increased political participation of the crowds and demographic fluctuations.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Christian communities and their doctrinal differences increasingly shaped the urban fabric.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The locus of power shifted to [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and to the [ascendant Islamic civilization](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") with its major cities [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), and [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain").[[83]](#cite_note-83) From the 9th through the end of the 12th century, [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), the capital of the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire"), was the largest and wealthiest city in Europe, with a population approaching 1 million.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) The [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") gradually gained [control over many cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_conquered_by_the_Ottoman_Empire "List of cities conquered by the Ottoman Empire") in the Mediterranean area, including [Constantinople in 1453](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople").

In the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), beginning in the 12th century, [free imperial cities](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") such as [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"), [Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich"), and [Nijmegen](/wiki/Nijmegen "Nijmegen") became a privileged elite among towns having won self-governance from their local lord or having been granted self-governance by the emperor and being placed under his immediate protection. By 1480, these cities, as far as still part of the empire, became part of the [Imperial Estates](/wiki/Imperial_Estates "Imperial Estates") governing the empire with the emperor through the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)").[[86]](#cite_note-86)

By the 13th and 14th centuries, some cities had become powerful states, taking surrounding areas under their control or establishing extensive maritime empires. In Italy, [medieval communes](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") developed into [city-states](/wiki/Italian_city-states "Italian city-states") including the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") and the [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa"). In Northern Europe, cities including [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") and [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") formed the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") for collective defense and commerce. Their power was later [challenged](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Hanseatic_War "Dutch–Hanseatic War") and eclipsed by the [Dutch](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") commercial [cities](/wiki/History_of_urban_centers_in_the_Low_Countries "History of urban centers in the Low Countries") of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres"), and [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam").[[87]](#cite_note-87) Similar phenomena existed elsewhere, as in the case of [Sakai](/wiki/Sakai,_Osaka "Sakai, Osaka"), which enjoyed considerable autonomy in late medieval Japan.

In the first millennium AD, the [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") capital of [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor") in Cambodia grew into the most extensive [preindustrial settlement](/wiki/Pre-industrial_society "Pre-industrial society") in the world by area,[[88]](#cite_note-Evans_PNAS-88)[[89]](#cite_note-BBC_News_2007-89) covering over 1,000 km2 (390 sq mi) and possibly supporting up to one million people.[[88]](#cite_note-Evans_PNAS-88)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") already had cities before the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), but the consolidation of [Trans-Saharan trade](/wiki/Trans-Saharan_trade "Trans-Saharan trade") in the Middle Ages multiplied the number of cities in the region, as well as making some of them very populous, notably [Gao](/wiki/Gao "Gao") (72,000 inhabitants in 800 AD), [Oyo-Ile](/wiki/Old_Oyo "Old Oyo") (50,000 inhabitants in 1400 AD, and may have reached up to 140,000 inhabitants in the 18th century), [Ile-Ifẹ̀](/wiki/If%E1%BA%B9%CC%80 "Ifẹ̀") (70,000 to 105,000 inhabitants in the 14th and 15th centuries), [Niani](/wiki/Niani,_Guinea "Niani, Guinea") (50,000 inhabitants in 1400 AD) and [Timbuktu](/wiki/Timbuktu "Timbuktu") (100,000 inhabitants in 1450 AD).[[72]](#cite_note-Monroe,_J._Cameron_2017-72)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Early modern")]

In the West, nation-states became the dominant unit of political organization following the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") in the seventeenth century.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-Blomley2013-93) Western Europe's larger capitals (London and Paris) benefited from the growth of commerce following the emergence of an [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") trade. However, most towns remained small.

During the Spanish colonization of the Americas, the old Roman city concept was extensively used. Cities were founded in the middle of the newly conquered territories and were bound to several laws regarding administration, finances, and urbanism.

### Industrial age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Industrial age")]

The [growth of the modern industry](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") from the late 18th century onward led to massive [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") and the rise of new great cities, first in Europe and then in other regions, as new opportunities brought huge numbers of migrants from rural communities into urban areas. England led the way as [London](/wiki/London "London") became the capital of a [world empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and cities across the country grew in locations strategic for [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing").[[94]](#cite_note-94) In the United States from 1860 to 1910, the [introduction of railroads](/wiki/History_of_rail_transport "History of rail transport") reduced transportation costs, and large manufacturing centers began to emerge, fueling migration from rural to city areas.

Some industrialized cities were confronted with health challenges associated with [overcrowding](/wiki/Overcrowding "Overcrowding"), [occupational hazards](/wiki/Occupational_hazard "Occupational hazard") of industry, contaminated water and air, [poor sanitation](/wiki/History_of_water_supply_and_sanitation#Modern_age "History of water supply and sanitation"), and communicable diseases such as [typhoid](/wiki/Typhoid "Typhoid") and [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"). [Factories](/wiki/Factories "Factories") and [slums](/wiki/Slum "Slum") emerged as regular features of the urban landscape.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Post-industrial age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Post-industrial age")]

In the second half of the 20th century, [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") (or "[economic restructuring](/wiki/Economic_restructuring "Economic restructuring")") in the West led to [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness"), and [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay") in formerly prosperous cities. America's "Steel Belt" became a "[Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt")" and cities such as [Detroit](/wiki/Decline_of_Detroit "Decline of Detroit"), Michigan, and [Gary, Indiana](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana") began to [shrink](/wiki/Shrinking_cities "Shrinking cities"), contrary to the global trend of massive urban expansion.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Such cities have shifted with varying success into the [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy") and [public-private partnerships](/wiki/Public-private_partnerships "Public-private partnerships"), with concomitant [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), uneven [revitalization efforts](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal"), and selective cultural development.[[97]](#cite_note-Kaplan164-97) Under the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") and subsequent [five-year plans](/wiki/Five-year_plans_of_China "Five-year plans of China") continuing today, [China](/wiki/China "China") has undergone concomitant [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization_in_China "Urbanization in China") and [industrialization](/wiki/Chinese_industrialization "Chinese industrialization") and become the world's leading [manufacturer](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing").[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Amidst these economic changes, [high technology](/wiki/High_technology "High technology") and instantaneous [telecommunication](/wiki/Telecommunication "Telecommunication") enable select cities to become centers of the [knowledge economy](/wiki/Knowledge_economy "Knowledge economy").[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) A new [smart city](/wiki/Smart_city "Smart city") paradigm, supported by institutions such as the [RAND Corporation](/wiki/RAND_Corporation "RAND Corporation") and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), is bringing computerized [surveillance](/wiki/Surveillance_issues_in_smart_cities "Surveillance issues in smart cities"), data analysis, and [governance](/wiki/E-governance "E-governance") to bear on cities and city dwellers.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Some companies are building brand-new [master-planned](/wiki/Land_use_planning "Land use planning") cities from scratch on [greenfield](/wiki/Greenfield_land "Greenfield land") sites.

## Urbanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Urbanization")]

Main article: [Urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Historical_global_urban_-_rural_population_trends.png/250px-Historical_global_urban_-_rural_population_trends.png)](/wiki/File:Historical_global_urban_-_rural_population_trends.png)

Graph showing urbanization from 1950 projected to 2050[[104]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png/250px-Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png)](/wiki/File:Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png)

Map showing urban areas with at least one million inhabitants in 2025

[Urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") is the process of migration from rural to urban areas, driven by various political, economic, and cultural factors. Until the 18th century, an equilibrium existed between the rural agricultural population and towns featuring [markets](/wiki/Market_(place) "Market (place)") and small-scale manufacturing.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-FreyZimmer2001-106) With the [agricultural](/wiki/British_Agricultural_Revolution "British Agricultural Revolution") and [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") revolutions, urban population began its unprecedented growth, both through migration and [demographic expansion](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition"). In [England](/wiki/England "England"), the proportion of the population living in cities jumped from 17% in 1801 to 72% in 1891.[[107]](#cite_note-urbanization-107) In 1900, 15% of the world's population lived in cities.[[108]](#cite_note-108) The cultural appeal of cities also plays a role in attracting residents.[[109]](#cite_note-MoholyNagy1968p136-109)

Urbanization rapidly spread across Europe and the Americas and since the 1950s has taken hold in Asia and Africa as well. The Population Division of the [United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs](/wiki/United_Nations_Department_of_Economic_and_Social_Affairs "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs") reported in 2014 that for the first time, more than half of the world population lives in cities.[[110]](#cite_note-SenguptaUN2014-110)[[a]](#cite_note-117)

[Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") is the most urban continent, with four-fifths of its population living in cities, including one-fifth of the population said to live in [shantytowns](/wiki/Shantytown "Shantytown") ([favelas](/wiki/Favela "Favela"), [poblaciones callampas](/wiki/Campamento_(Chile) "Campamento (Chile)"), etc.).[[117]](#cite_note-118) [Batam](/wiki/Batam "Batam"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Mogadishu](/wiki/Mogadishu "Mogadishu"), [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), and [Niamey](/wiki/Niamey "Niamey"), [Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger"), are considered among the world's fastest-growing cities, with annual growth rates of 5–8%.[[118]](#cite_note-119) In general, the [more developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") of the "[Global North](/wiki/Global_North "Global North")" remain more urbanized than the [less developed countries](/wiki/Less_developed_countries "Less developed countries") of the "[Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South")"—but the difference continues to shrink because urbanization is happening faster in the latter group. Asia is home to by far the greatest absolute number of city-dwellers: over two billion and counting.[[106]](#cite_note-FreyZimmer2001-106) The UN predicts an additional 2.5 billion city dwellers (and 300 million fewer country dwellers) worldwide by 2050, with 90% of urban population expansion occurring in Asia and Africa.[[110]](#cite_note-SenguptaUN2014-110)[[119]](#cite_note-120)

[Megacities](/wiki/Megacities "Megacities"), cities with populations in the multi-millions, have proliferated into the dozens, arising especially in Asia, Africa, and Latin America.[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122) Economic globalization fuels the growth of these cities, as new torrents of foreign [capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital") arrange for rapid industrialization, as well as the [relocation of major businesses](/wiki/Offshoring "Offshoring") from Europe and North America, attracting [immigrants](/wiki/Immigrant "Immigrant") from near and far.[[122]](#cite_note-123) A deep gulf divides the rich and poor in these cities, which usually contain a super-wealthy elite living in [gated communities](/wiki/Gated_community "Gated community") and large masses of people living in substandard housing with inadequate infrastructure and otherwise poor conditions.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

Cities around the world have expanded physically as they grow in population, with increases in their surface extent, with the creation of high-rise buildings for residential and commercial use, and with development underground.[[124]](#cite_note-125)[[125]](#cite_note-126)

Urbanization can create rapid demand for [water resources management](/wiki/Water_resources_management "Water resources management"), as formerly good sources of freshwater become overused and polluted, and the volume of [sewage](/wiki/Sewage "Sewage") begins to exceed manageable levels.[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Government")]

Further information: [Local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/City_Council_of_Tehran%2C_17_September_2015.jpg/250px-City_Council_of_Tehran%2C_17_September_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Council_of_Tehran,_17_September_2015.jpg)

The city council of [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran") meets in September 2015

[The local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") of cities takes different forms including prominently the [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") (especially [in England](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England"), [in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States"), [India](/wiki/Municipal_governance_in_India "Municipal governance in India"), and former [British colonies](/wiki/Crown_colonies "Crown colonies"); legally, the [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation");[[127]](#cite_note-128) *[municipio](/wiki/Municipio "Municipio")* in [Spain](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Spain "Municipalities of Spain") and [Portugal](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Portugal "Municipalities of Portugal"), and, along with *[municipalidad](/wiki/Municipalidad "Municipalidad")*, in most former parts of the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") empires) and the *commune* ([in France](/wiki/Communes_in_France "Communes in France") and [Chile](/wiki/Communes_of_Chile "Communes of Chile"); or *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* in Italy).

The chief official of the city is very often called the "[mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor")". Whatever their true degree of political authority, the mayor typically acts as the [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead") or personification of their city.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

Legal conflicts and issues arise more frequently in cities than elsewhere due to the bare fact of their greater density.[[129]](#cite_note-130) Modern city governments thoroughly [regulate](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation") [everyday life](/wiki/Everyday_life "Everyday life") in many dimensions, including [public](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") and personal [health](/wiki/Health "Health"), [transport](/wiki/Transport "Transport"), [burial](/wiki/Burial "Burial"), [resource](/wiki/Resource "Resource") use and [extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction"), [recreation](/wiki/Recreation "Recreation"), and the nature and use of [buildings](/wiki/Building "Building"). Technologies, techniques, and laws governing these areas—developed in cities—have become ubiquitous in many areas.[[130]](#cite_note-131)
Municipal officials may be appointed from a higher level of government or elected locally.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

### Municipal services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Municipal services")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Aftermath_of_a_huge_fire_at_Thomas_McKenzie_%26_Sons_Ltd._on_Pearse_Street%2C_Dublin.jpg/250px-Aftermath_of_a_huge_fire_at_Thomas_McKenzie_%26_Sons_Ltd._on_Pearse_Street%2C_Dublin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aftermath_of_a_huge_fire_at_Thomas_McKenzie_%26_Sons_Ltd._on_Pearse_Street,_Dublin.jpg)

The [Dublin Fire Brigade](/wiki/Dublin_Fire_Brigade "Dublin Fire Brigade") in Dublin, Ireland, extinguishing a severe fire at a hardware store in 1970

Cities typically provide [municipal services](/wiki/Municipal_services "Municipal services") such as [education](/wiki/Education "Education"), through [school systems](/wiki/School_system "School system"); [policing](/wiki/Police "Police"), through police departments; and [firefighting](/wiki/Firefighting "Firefighting"), through [fire departments](/wiki/Fire_department "Fire department"); as well as the city's basic infrastructure. These are provided more or less routinely, in a more or less equal fashion.[[132]](#cite_note-JonesEtAl1980-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) Responsibility for administration usually falls on the city government, but some services may be operated by a higher level of government,[[134]](#cite_note-135) while others may be privately run.[[135]](#cite_note-136) Armies may assume responsibility for policing cities in states of domestic turmoil such as America's [King assassination riots](/wiki/King_assassination_riots "King assassination riots") of 1968.

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Finance")]

The traditional basis for municipal finance is local [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") levied on [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") within the city. Local government can also collect revenue for services, or by leasing land that it owns.[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137) However, financing municipal services, as well as [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") and other development projects, is a perennial problem, which cities address through appeals to higher governments, arrangements with the private sector, and techniques such as [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization") (selling services into the [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector")), [corporatization](/wiki/Corporatization "Corporatization") (formation of quasi-private municipally owned corporations), and [financialization](/wiki/Financialization "Financialization") (packaging city assets into tradeable financial public contracts and other related rights). This situation has become acute in deindustrialized cities and in cases where businesses and wealthier citizens have moved outside of [city limits](/wiki/City_limits "City limits") and therefore beyond the reach of taxation.[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-Weber2010-141) Cities in search of [ready cash](/wiki/Cash_and_cash_equivalents "Cash and cash equivalents") increasingly resort to the [municipal bond](/wiki/Municipal_bond "Municipal bond"), essentially a loan with [interest](/wiki/Maturity_(finance) "Maturity (finance)") and a [repayment date](/wiki/Maturity_(finance) "Maturity (finance)").[[141]](#cite_note-142) City governments have also begun to use [tax increment financing](/wiki/Tax_increment_financing "Tax increment financing"), in which a development project is financed by loans based on future tax revenues which it is expected to yield.[[140]](#cite_note-Weber2010-141) Under these circumstances, creditors and consequently city governments place a high importance on city [credit ratings](/wiki/Credit_rating "Credit rating").[[142]](#cite_note-143)

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg/250px-Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ripon_Building_panorama.jpg)

The [Ripon Building](/wiki/Ripon_Building "Ripon Building") is the headquarters of [Greater Chennai Corporation](/wiki/Greater_Chennai_Corporation "Greater Chennai Corporation") in Chennai, one of the oldest city-governing corporations in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").

[Governance](/wiki/Governance "Governance") includes government but refers to a wider domain of [social control](/wiki/Social_control "Social control") functions implemented by many actors including [non-governmental organizations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization").[[143]](#cite_note-144) The impact of globalization and the role of [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") in local governments worldwide have led to a shift in perspective on urban governance, away from the "urban regime theory" in which a coalition of local interests functionally govern, toward a theory of outside economic control, widely associated in academics with the philosophy of [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009142–143-145) In the neoliberal model of governance, public utilities are [privatized](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization"), the industry is [deregulated](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"), and [corporations](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation") gain the status of governing actors—as indicated by the power they wield in [public-private partnerships](/wiki/Public-private_partnerships "Public-private partnerships") and over [business improvement districts](/wiki/Business_improvement_districts "Business improvement districts"), and in the expectation of self-regulation through [corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility"). The biggest [investors](/wiki/Investor "Investor") and [real estate developers](/wiki/Real_estate_developer "Real estate developer") act as the city's [de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto") urban planners.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

The related concept of [good governance](/wiki/Good_governance "Good governance") places more emphasis on the state, with the purpose of assessing urban governments for their suitability for [development assistance](/wiki/Development_assistance "Development assistance").[[146]](#cite_note-Gupta2015p33-147) The concepts of governance and good governance are especially invoked in emergent megacities, where international organizations consider existing governments inadequate for their large populations.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

### Urban planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Urban planning")]

Main articles: [Urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning") and [Urban design](/wiki/Urban_design "Urban design")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/La_Plata_desde_el_aire.JPG/250px-La_Plata_desde_el_aire.JPG)](/wiki/File:La_Plata_desde_el_aire.JPG)

[La Plata](/wiki/La_Plata "La Plata") in Argentina is based on a perfect square with 5196-meter sides, and was designed in the 1880s as the new capital of [Buenos Aires Province](/wiki/Buenos_Aires_Province "Buenos Aires Province").[[148]](#cite_note-149)

[Urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), the application of forethought to city design, involves optimizing land use, transportation, utilities, and other basic systems in order to achieve [certain objectives](/wiki/Technical_aspects_of_urban_planning "Technical aspects of urban planning"). Urban planners and scholars have proposed overlapping [theories](/wiki/Theories_of_urban_planning "Theories of urban planning") as ideals of how plans should be formed. Planning tools, beyond the original design of the city itself, include [public capital](/wiki/Public_capital "Public capital") investment in infrastructure and [land-use controls](/wiki/Land-use_planning "Land-use planning") such as [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning"). The continuous process of [comprehensive planning](/wiki/Comprehensive_planning "Comprehensive planning") involves identifying general objectives as well as collecting data to evaluate progress and inform future decisions.[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-McQuillin1987planning-151)

Government is legally the final authority on planning but in practice, the process involves both public and private elements. The legal principle of [eminent domain](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain") is used by the government to divest citizens of their property in cases where its use is required for a project.[[150]](#cite_note-McQuillin1987planning-151) Planning often involves tradeoffs—decisions in which some stand to gain and some to lose—and thus is closely connected to the prevailing political situation.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

The [history of urban planning](/wiki/History_of_urban_planning "History of urban planning") dates back to some of the earliest known cities, especially in the Indus Valley and Mesoamerican civilizations, which built their cities on grids and apparently zoned different areas for different purposes.[[15]](#cite_note-Smith-15)[[152]](#cite_note-153) The effects of planning, ubiquitous in today's world, can be seen most clearly in the layout of [planned communities](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community"), fully designed prior to construction, often with consideration for interlocking physical, economic, and cultural systems.

## Society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Society")]

### Social structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Social structure")]

[Urban society](/wiki/Urban_sociology "Urban sociology") is typically [stratified](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification"). Spatially, cities are formally or informally [segregated](/wiki/Geographical_segregation "Geographical segregation") along ethnic, economic, and racial lines. People living relatively close together may live, work, and play in separate areas, and associate with different people, forming [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") or [lifestyle](/wiki/Lifestyle_enclave "Lifestyle enclave") enclaves or, in areas of concentrated poverty, [ghettoes](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto"). While in the US and elsewhere poverty became associated with the [inner city](/wiki/Inner_city "Inner city"), in France it has become associated with the *[banlieues](/wiki/Banlieue "Banlieue")*, areas of urban development that surround the city proper. Meanwhile, across Europe and North America, the racially [white](/wiki/White_people "White people") majority is empirically the most segregated group. [Suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") in the West, and, increasingly, [gated communities](/wiki/Gated_community "Gated community") and other forms of "privatopia" around the world, allow local elites to self-segregate into secure and exclusive [neighborhoods](/wiki/Neighborhood "Neighborhood").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009131–140-154)

Landless urban workers, contrasted with [peasants](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") and known as the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat"), form a growing stratum of society in the age of urbanization. In [Marxist](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") doctrine, the proletariat will inevitably [revolt](/wiki/Proletarian_revolution "Proletarian revolution") against the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") as their ranks swell with disenfranchised and disaffected people lacking all stake[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the [status quo](/wiki/Status_quo "Status quo").[[154]](#cite_note-155) The global urban proletariat of today, however, generally lacks the status of factory workers which in the nineteenth century provided access to the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production").[[155]](#cite_note-Davis2004-156)

### Economics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Economics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Taipei_Skyline_2022.06.29.jpg/250px-Taipei_Skyline_2022.06.29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taipei_Skyline_2022.06.29.jpg)

Clusters of skyscrapers in [Xinyi Planning District](/wiki/Xinyi_Planning_District "Xinyi Planning District"), the centre of commerce and finance of [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), the capital of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")

Historically, cities rely on [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area") for [intensive farming](/wiki/Intensive_farming "Intensive farming") to [yield surplus crops](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield"), in exchange for which they provide money, political administration, manufactured goods, and culture.[[28]](#cite_note-Kaplan155-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Marshall-29) [Urban economics](/wiki/Urban_economics "Urban economics") tends to analyze larger agglomerations, stretching beyond city limits, in order to reach a more complete understanding of the local [labor market](/wiki/Labor_market "Labor market").[[156]](#cite_note-157)

As hubs of trade, cities have long been home to [retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail") commerce and [consumption](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)") through the interface of [shopping](/wiki/Shopping "Shopping"). In the 20th century, [department stores](/wiki/Department_store "Department store") using new techniques of [advertising](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising"), [public relations](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations"), [decoration](/wiki/Decorative_arts "Decorative arts"), and [design](/wiki/Design "Design"), transformed urban shopping areas into [fantasy worlds](/wiki/Fantasy_world "Fantasy world") encouraging self-expression and escape through [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism").[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)

In general, the density of cities expedites commerce and facilitates [knowledge spillovers](/wiki/Knowledge_spillover "Knowledge spillover"), helping people and firms exchange information and generate new ideas.[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161) A thicker labor market allows for better skill matching between firms and individuals. Population density also enables sharing of common infrastructure and production facilities; however, in very dense cities, increased crowding and waiting times may lead to some negative effects.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

Although [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") fueled the growth of cities, many now rely on a [tertiary](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") or [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy"). The services in question range from [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry"), [entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment "Entertainment"), and [housekeeping](/wiki/Housekeeping "Housekeeping") to [grey-collar](/wiki/Grey-collar "Grey-collar") work in [law](/wiki/Legal_outsourcing "Legal outsourcing"), financial consulting, and [administration](/wiki/Management "Management").[[97]](#cite_note-Kaplan164-97)[[162]](#cite_note-163)

According to a scientific model of cities by Professor [Geoffrey West](/wiki/Geoffrey_West "Geoffrey West"), with the doubling of a city's size, salaries per capita will generally increase by 15%.[[163]](#cite_note-164)

### Culture and communications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Culture and communications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Paris_-_Eiffelturm_und_Marsfeld2.jpg/250px-Paris_-_Eiffelturm_und_Marsfeld2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_-_Eiffelturm_und_Marsfeld2.jpg)

[Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") is one of the best-known cities in the world.[[164]](#cite_note-165)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Nepalese_Dancers_at_Heritage_Days%2C_Edmonton.jpg/250px-Nepalese_Dancers_at_Heritage_Days%2C_Edmonton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nepalese_Dancers_at_Heritage_Days,_Edmonton.jpg)

[Nepalese](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") dancers at [Edmonton Heritage Festival](/wiki/Edmonton_Heritage_Festival "Edmonton Heritage Festival"), in [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), Canada, an example of the cultural diversity of a city

Cities are typically hubs for [education](/wiki/Education "Education") and [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts"), supporting [universities](/wiki/University "University"), [museums](/wiki/Museum "Museum"), [temples](/wiki/Temple "Temple"), and other [cultural institutions](/wiki/Cultural_institutions "Cultural institutions").[[165]](#cite_note-Marshall14-166) They feature impressive displays of [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") ranging from small to enormous and ornate to [brutal](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture"); [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper"), providing thousands of offices or homes within a small footprint, and visible from miles away, have become iconic urban features.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200984–85-167) Cultural elites tend to live in cities, bound together by shared [cultural capital](/wiki/Cultural_capital "Cultural capital"), and themselves play some role in governance.[[167]](#cite_note-168) By virtue of their status as centers of culture and literacy, cities can be described as the locus of [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization"), [human history](/wiki/Human_history "Human history"), and [social change](/wiki/Social_change "Social change").[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)

Density makes for effective [mass communication](/wiki/Mass_communication "Mass communication") and transmission of [news](/wiki/News "News"), through [heralds](/wiki/Herald "Herald"), printed [proclamations](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation"), [newspapers](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper"), and digital media. These communication networks, though still using cities as hubs, penetrate extensively into all populated areas. In the age of rapid communication and transportation, commentators have described urban culture as nearly ubiquitous[[170]](#cite_note-HugoEtAl2003-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173) or as no longer meaningful.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

Today, a city's promotion of its cultural activities dovetails with [place branding](/wiki/Place_branding "Place branding") and [city marketing](/wiki/City_marketing "City marketing"), [public diplomacy](/wiki/Public_diplomacy "Public diplomacy") techniques used to inform development strategy; attract businesses, investors, residents, and tourists; and create [shared identity](/wiki/Collective_identity "Collective identity") and [sense of place](/wiki/Sense_of_place "Sense of place") within the metropolitan area.[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[175]](#cite_note-Wachsmuth2014-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-KerrOliver2015-178) Physical inscriptions, [plaques](/wiki/Historical_marker "Historical marker"), and [monuments](/wiki/Monument "Monument") on display physically transmit a historical context for urban places.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009186–189-179) Some cities, such as [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") have indelible religious status and for hundreds of years have attracted [pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim "Pilgrim"). Patriotic tourists visit [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") to see the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal"), or [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") to visit the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present) "World Trade Center (2001–present)"). [Elvis](/wiki/Elvis "Elvis") lovers visit [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee") to pay their respects at [Graceland](/wiki/Graceland "Graceland").[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200941,_189–192-180) Place brands (which include place satisfaction and place loyalty) have great economic value (comparable to the value of commodity [brands](/wiki/Brand "Brand")) because of their influence on the [decision-making](/wiki/Decision-making "Decision-making") process of people thinking about doing business in—"purchasing" (the brand of)—a city.[[177]](#cite_note-KerrOliver2015-178)

[Bread and circuses](/wiki/Bread_and_circuses "Bread and circuses") among other forms of cultural appeal, attract and entertain [the masses](/wiki/Commoner "Commoner").[[109]](#cite_note-MoholyNagy1968p136-109)[[180]](#cite_note-181) Sports also play a major role in city branding and local [identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)") formation.[[181]](#cite_note-182) Cities go to considerable lengths in competing to host the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), which bring global attention and tourism.[[182]](#cite_note-Ward2008-183) Paris, a city known for its cultural history, was the site of the most recent Olympics in the summer of 2024.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

### Warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Warfare")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/The_Second_World_War_1939-45-_Victory_and_Aftermath_IND5196.jpg/250px-The_Second_World_War_1939-45-_Victory_and_Aftermath_IND5196.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Second_World_War_1939-45-_Victory_and_Aftermath_IND5196.jpg)

The [atomic bombing](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") of [Hiroshima](/wiki/Hiroshima "Hiroshima") on 6 August 1945 devastated the city and led to [Imperial Japan's surrender](/wiki/Japanese_Instrument_of_Surrender "Japanese Instrument of Surrender") and the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Cities play a crucial strategic role in warfare due to their economic, demographic, symbolic, and political centrality. For the same reasons, they are targets in [asymmetric warfare](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare"). Many cities throughout history were founded under military auspices, a great many have incorporated [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification"), and military principles continue to [influence urban design](/wiki/Military_urbanism "Military urbanism").[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009127–128-185) Indeed, war may have served as the social rationale and economic basis for the very earliest cities.[[51]](#cite_note-Mumfurd1961war-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Ashworth1991p12-52)
