Powers engaged in [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitics "Geopolitics") conflict have established fortified settlements as part of military strategies, as in the case of [garrison](/wiki/Garrison "Garrison") towns, America's [Strategic Hamlet Program](/wiki/Strategic_Hamlet_Program "Strategic Hamlet Program") during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") in Palestine.[[185]](#cite_note-186) While [occupying](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War") the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the US Army ordered local people to concentrate in cities and towns, in order to isolate committed insurgents and battle freely against them in the countryside.[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), national governments on occasion declared certain cities [open](/wiki/Open_city "Open city"), effectively [surrendering](/wiki/Surrender_(military) "Surrender (military)") them to an advancing enemy in order to avoid damage and bloodshed. Urban warfare proved decisive, however, in the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"), where Soviet forces repulsed German occupiers, with extreme casualties and destruction. In an era of [low-intensity conflict](/wiki/Low-intensity_conflict "Low-intensity conflict") and rapid urbanization, cities have become sites of long-term conflict waged both by foreign occupiers and by local governments against [insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency").[[155]](#cite_note-Davis2004-156)[[188]](#cite_note-189) Such warfare, known as [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency"), involves techniques of surveillance and [psychological warfare](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare") as well as [close combat](/wiki/Close_combat "Close combat"),[[189]](#cite_note-190) and functionally extends modern urban [crime prevention](/wiki/Crime_prevention "Crime prevention"), which already uses concepts such as [defensible space](/wiki/Defensible_space_theory "Defensible space theory").[[190]](#cite_note-191)

Although capture is the more common objective, warfare has in some cases spelled complete destruction for a city. Mesopotamian [tablets](/wiki/Cuneiform_script#List_of_major_cuneiform_tablet_discoveries "Cuneiform script") and [ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins") attest to such destruction,[[191]](#cite_note-192) as does the Latin motto *[Carthago delenda est](/wiki/Carthago_delenda_est "Carthago delenda est")*.[[192]](#cite_note-193)[[193]](#cite_note-194) Since the [atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") and throughout the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), [nuclear strategists](/wiki/Nuclear_strategy "Nuclear strategy") continued to contemplate the use of "[counter-value](/wiki/Countervalue "Countervalue")" targeting: crippling an enemy by annihilating its valuable cities, rather than [aiming primarily at its military forces](/wiki/Counterforce "Counterforce").[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196)

### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Climate change")]

Main article: [Climate change and cities](/wiki/Climate_change_and_cities "Climate change and cities")

This section is an excerpt from [Climate change and cities](/wiki/Climate_change_and_cities "Climate change and cities").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Climate_change_and_cities&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Jakarta_Panorama.jpg/330px-Jakarta_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_Panorama.jpg)

[Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") was listed as the most vulnerable city to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Indonesia "Climate change in Indonesia") in a 2021 [Verisk Maplecroft](/wiki/Verisk_Maplecroft "Verisk Maplecroft") study.[[196]](#cite_note-197)

[Climate change and cities](/wiki/Climate_change_and_cities "Climate change and cities") are deeply connected. Cities are one of the greatest contributors and likely best opportunities for addressing [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[197]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:4-198) Cities are also one of the most vulnerable parts of the human society to the [effects of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change"),[[198]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:5-199) and likely one of the most important solutions for reducing the [environmental impact of humans](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment").[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[197]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:4-198)[[198]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:5-199) The UN projects that 68% of the world population will live in urban areas by 2050.[[200]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:9-201) In the year 2016, 31 mega-cities reported having at least 10 million in their population, 8 of which surpassed 20 million people.[[201]](#cite_note-202) However, [secondary cities](/wiki/Secondary_city "Secondary city") - small to medium size cities (500,000 to 1 million) are rapidly increasing in number and are some of the fastest growing urbanizing areas in the world further contributing to climate change impacts.[[202]](#cite_note-203) Cities have a significant influence on construction and transportation—two of the key contributors to global warming emissions.[[203]](#cite_note-Climate_change_and_cities_:6-204) Moreover, because of processes that create [climate conflict](/wiki/Climate_conflict "Climate conflict") and [climate refugees](/wiki/Environmental_migrant "Environmental migrant"), city areas are expected to grow during the next several decades, stressing infrastructure and [concentrating more impoverished peoples in cities](/wiki/Climate_change_and_poverty "Climate change and poverty").[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Kannengie%C3%9Ferbr%C3%BCcke_Drought_2022.jpg/330px-Kannengie%C3%9Ferbr%C3%BCcke_Drought_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kannengie%C3%9Ferbr%C3%BCcke_Drought_2022.jpg)

[Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), is a large city that has experienced multiple droughts throughout the years, which has led to decreased economic productivity.[[206]](#cite_note-207)

High density and [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") effect are examples of weather changes that impact cities due to climate change. It also causes exacerbating existing problems such as air pollution, [water scarcity](/wiki/Water_scarcity "Water scarcity"), and heat illness in metropolitan areas. Moreover, because most cities have been built on rivers or coastal areas, cities are frequently vulnerable to the subsequent effects of [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"), which cause [flooding](/w/index.php?title=C40_Cities_Climate_Leadership_Grouptal&action=edit&redlink=1 "C40 Cities Climate Leadership Grouptal (page does not exist)") and [erosion](/wiki/Coastal_erosion "Coastal erosion"); these effects are also connected with other urban environmental problems, such as [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") and [aquifer depletion](/wiki/Aquifer_recharge "Aquifer recharge").

A report by the [C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group](/wiki/C40_Cities_Climate_Leadership_Group "C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group") described [consumption based emissions](/wiki/Consumption-based_emissions_of_greenhouse_gases "Consumption-based emissions of greenhouse gases") as having significantly more impact than production-based emissions within cities. The report estimates that 85% of the emissions associated with goods within a city is generated outside of that city.[[207]](#cite_note-208) [Climate change adaptation](/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation") and [mitigation investments in cities](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") will be important in reducing the impacts of some of the largest contributors of greenhouse gas emissions: for example, increased density allows for redistribution of [land use for agriculture](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_agriculture "Effects of climate change on agriculture") and [reforestation](/wiki/Reforestation "Reforestation"), [improving transportation efficiencies](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_transport "Environmental impact of transport"), and greening construction ([largely due to cement's outsized role in climate change](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_concrete "Environmental impact of concrete") and improvements in [sustainable construction practices](/wiki/Sustainability_in_construction "Sustainability in construction") and [weatherization](/wiki/Weatherization "Weatherization")).

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Traffic_congestion_Jl_Asia_Afrika_Bandung.jpg/250px-Traffic_congestion_Jl_Asia_Afrika_Bandung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traffic_congestion_Jl_Asia_Afrika_Bandung.jpg)

Traffic congestion in [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung "Bandung") in Indonesia

Urban [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") involves various physical networks and spaces necessary for transportation, water use, energy, recreation, and public functions.[[208]](#cite_note-Tarr-209) Infrastructure carries a high initial cost in [fixed capital](/wiki/Fixed_capital "Fixed capital") but lower [marginal costs](/wiki/Marginal_cost "Marginal cost") and thus positive [economies of scale](/wiki/Economies_of_scale "Economies of scale").[[209]](#cite_note-WellmanSpiller2012-210) Because of the higher [barriers to entry](/wiki/Barriers_to_entry "Barriers to entry"), these networks have been classified as [natural monopolies](/wiki/Natural_monopoly "Natural monopoly"), meaning that economic logic favors control of each network by a single organization, public or private.[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127)[[210]](#cite_note-WellmanPretorius2012-211)

Infrastructure in general plays a vital role in a city's capacity for economic activity and expansion, underpinning the very survival of the city's inhabitants, as well as technological, commercial, industrial, and social activities.[[208]](#cite_note-Tarr-209)[[209]](#cite_note-WellmanSpiller2012-210) Structurally, many infrastructure systems take the form of [networks](/wiki/Network_theory "Network theory") with redundant links and multiple pathways, so that the system as a whole continues to operate even if parts of it fail.[[210]](#cite_note-WellmanPretorius2012-211) The particulars of a city's infrastructure systems have historical [path dependence](/wiki/Path_dependence "Path dependence") because new development must build from what exists already.[[209]](#cite_note-WellmanSpiller2012-210)

[Megaprojects](/wiki/Megaproject "Megaproject") such as the construction of [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport"), [power plants](/wiki/Power_plant "Power plant"), and [railways](/wiki/Railway "Railway") require large upfront investments and thus tend to require funding from the national government or the private sector.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200970-212)[[210]](#cite_note-WellmanPretorius2012-211) Privatization may also extend to all levels of infrastructure construction and maintenance.[[212]](#cite_note-213)

Urban infrastructure ideally serves all residents equally but in practice may prove uneven—with, in some cities, clear first-class and second-class alternatives.[[213]](#cite_note-Lineberry-214)[[214]](#cite_note-215)[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/AcueductoSegovia04.JPG/250px-AcueductoSegovia04.JPG)](/wiki/File:AcueductoSegovia04.JPG)

[Aqueduct of Segovia](/wiki/Aqueduct_of_Segovia "Aqueduct of Segovia") in Segovia, Spain

[Public utilities](/wiki/Public_utility "Public utility") (literally, useful things with general availability) include basic and essential infrastructure networks, chiefly concerned with the supply of water, electricity, and telecommunications capability to the populace.[[215]](#cite_note-216)

[Sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation"), necessary for good health in crowded conditions, requires water supply and [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management") as well as individual [hygiene](/wiki/Hygiene "Hygiene"). Urban [water](/wiki/Water "Water") systems include principally a [water supply network](/wiki/Water_supply_network "Water supply network") and a network ([sewerage system](/wiki/Sewerage "Sewerage")) for [sewage](/wiki/Sewage "Sewage") and [stormwater](/wiki/Stormwater "Stormwater"). [Historically](/wiki/History_of_water_supply_and_sanitation "History of water supply and sanitation"), either local governments or private companies have administered urban [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply"), with a tendency toward government water supply in the 20th century and a tendency toward private operation at the turn of the twenty-first.[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127)[[b]](#cite_note-218) The market for private water services is dominated by two French companies, [Veolia Water](/wiki/Veolia_Water "Veolia Water") (formerly [Vivendi](/wiki/Vivendi "Vivendi")) and [Engie](/wiki/Engie "Engie") (formerly [Suez](/wiki/Suez_(company,_1997%E2%80%932008) "Suez (company, 1997–2008)")), said to hold 70% of all water contracts worldwide.[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127)[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Modern urban life relies heavily on the [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") transmitted through [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") for the operation of [electric machines](/wiki/Electric_machines "Electric machines") (from household [appliances](/wiki/Home_appliance "Home appliance") to [industrial machines](/wiki/Outline_of_industrial_machinery "Outline of industrial machinery") to now-ubiquitous [electronic](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") systems used in communications, business, and government) and for [traffic lights](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light"), [street lights](/wiki/Street_light "Street light"), and indoor [lighting](/wiki/Lighting "Lighting"). Cities rely to a lesser extent on [hydrocarbon fuels](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_fuel "Hydrocarbon fuel") such as [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") for transportation, [heating](/wiki/Heating "Heating"), and [cooking](/wiki/Cooking "Cooking"). [Telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") infrastructure such as [telephone lines](/wiki/Telephone_line "Telephone line") and [coaxial cables](/wiki/Coaxial_cable "Coaxial cable") also traverse cities, forming dense networks for [mass](/wiki/Mass_communication "Mass communication") and [point-to-point](/wiki/Point-to-point_(telecommunications) "Point-to-point (telecommunications)") communications.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009169–170-220)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Transportation")]

See also: [Public transport](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Gautrain...%2C_O_R_Tambo_Intl_Airport_South_Africa.jpg/250px-Gautrain...%2C_O_R_Tambo_Intl_Airport_South_Africa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gautrain...,_O_R_Tambo_Intl_Airport_South_Africa.jpg)

[Gautrain](/wiki/Gautrain "Gautrain") at [O. R. Tambo International Airport](/wiki/O._R._Tambo_International_Airport "O. R. Tambo International Airport") in Johannesburg

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Harmoni_Central_Busway_Transjakarta_4.JPG/250px-Harmoni_Central_Busway_Transjakarta_4.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harmoni_Central_Busway_Transjakarta_4.JPG)

[TransJakarta](/wiki/TransJakarta "TransJakarta") in Indonesia is the longest [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") system in the world.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Kevyen_liikenteen_v%C3%A4yl%C3%A4_Baana_-_G8541_-_hkm.HKMS000005-km0000n5jx.jpg/250px-Kevyen_liikenteen_v%C3%A4yl%C3%A4_Baana_-_G8541_-_hkm.HKMS000005-km0000n5jx.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kevyen_liikenteen_v%C3%A4yl%C3%A4_Baana_-_G8541_-_hkm.HKMS000005-km0000n5jx.jpg)

[Baana](/wiki/Baana "Baana"), a [shared-path](/wiki/Shared-use_path "Shared-use path") [rail trail](/wiki/Rail_trail "Rail trail") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki")

Because cities rely on specialization and an [economic system](/wiki/Economic_system "Economic system") based on [wage labor](/wiki/Wage_labour "Wage labour"), their inhabitants must have the ability to regularly travel between home, work, commerce, and entertainment.[[219]](#cite_note-221) City dwellers travel by foot or by wheel on [roads](/wiki/Road "Road") and [walkways](/wiki/Walkway "Walkway"), or use special [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") systems based on [underground](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel"), [overground](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail"), and [elevated](/wiki/Elevated_railway "Elevated railway") rail. Cities also rely on long-distance transportation (truck, [rail](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport"), and [airplane](/wiki/Airplane "Airplane")) for economic connections with other cities and rural areas.[[220]](#cite_note-Hart2001-222)

City streets historically were the domain of [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") and their riders and [pedestrians](/wiki/Pedestrian "Pedestrian"), who only sometimes had [sidewalks](/wiki/Sidewalk "Sidewalk") and [special walking areas](/wiki/Transit_mall "Transit mall") reserved for them.[[221]](#cite_note-223) In the West, [bicycles](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle") or ([velocipedes](/wiki/Velocipede "Velocipede")), efficient human-powered machines for short- and medium-distance travel,[[222]](#cite_note-224) enjoyed a period of popularity at the beginning of the twentieth century before the rise of automobiles.[[223]](#cite_note-225) Soon after, they gained a more lasting foothold in Asian and African cities under European influence.[[224]](#cite_note-226) In Western cities, industrializing, [electrifying](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification"), and expanding [public transit](/wiki/Public_transit "Public transit") systems, especially [streetcars](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar"), enabled urban expansion as new residential neighborhoods sprang up along transit lines and workers rode to and from work downtown.[[220]](#cite_note-Hart2001-222)[[225]](#cite_note-WhittYago1985-227)

Since the mid-20th century, cities have relied heavily on [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") transportation, with major [implications](/wiki/Effects_of_the_car_on_societies "Effects of the car on societies") for their layout, environment, and aesthetics.[[226]](#cite_note-Borden-228) (This transformation occurred most dramatically in the US—where corporate and governmental policies favored automobile transport systems—and to a lesser extent in Europe.)[[220]](#cite_note-Hart2001-222)[[225]](#cite_note-WhittYago1985-227) The rise of personal [cars](/wiki/Car "Car") accompanied the expansion of urban economic areas into much larger [metropolises](/wiki/Metropolis "Metropolis"), subsequently creating ubiquitous [traffic](/wiki/Traffic "Traffic") issues with the accompanying construction of new [highways](/wiki/Highway "Highway"), wider streets, and alternative [walkways](/wiki/Walkway "Walkway") for pedestrians.[[227]](#cite_note-229)[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200930–32-231)[[175]](#cite_note-Wachsmuth2014-176) However, severe traffic jams still occur regularly in cities around the world, as private car ownership and urbanization continue to increase, overwhelming existing urban [street networks](/wiki/Street_network "Street network").[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137)

The urban [bus system](/wiki/Public_transport_bus_service "Public transport bus service"), the world's most common form of public transport, uses a network of scheduled [routes](/wiki/Bus_route "Bus route") to move people through the city, alongside cars, on the roads.[[230]](#cite_note-232) The economic function itself also became more decentralized as concentration became impractical and employers relocated to more car-friendly locations (including [edge cities](/wiki/Edge_city "Edge city")).[[220]](#cite_note-Hart2001-222) Some cities have introduced [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") systems which include exclusive [bus lanes](/wiki/Bus_lanes "Bus lanes") and other methods for prioritizing bus traffic over private cars.[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137)[[231]](#cite_note-233) Many big American cities still operate conventional public transit by rail, as exemplified by the ever-popular [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") system. Rapid transit is widely used in Europe and has increased in Latin America and Asia.[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137)

[Walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking") and [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") ("non-motorized transport") enjoy increasing favor (more [pedestrian zones](/wiki/Pedestrian_zone "Pedestrian zone") and [bike lanes](/wiki/Bike_lane "Bike lane")) in American and Asian urban transportation planning, under the influence of such trends as the [Healthy Cities](/wiki/Healthy_city "Healthy city") movement, the drive for [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"), and the idea of a [carfree city](/wiki/Carfree_city "Carfree city").[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137)[[232]](#cite_note-234)[[233]](#cite_note-235) Techniques such as [road space rationing](/wiki/Road_space_rationing "Road space rationing") and [road use charges](/wiki/Road_pricing "Road pricing") have been introduced to limit urban car traffic.[[136]](#cite_note-Gwilliam2013-137)

## Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Housing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Winsbury_Terrace_75-79_Kent_Street_Millers_Point.jpg/250px-Winsbury_Terrace_75-79_Kent_Street_Millers_Point.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winsbury_Terrace_75-79_Kent_Street_Millers_Point.jpg)

Horbury Terrace, a [terrace housing](/wiki/Terrace_house "Terrace house") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), c. 1836

[The housing](/wiki/Housing "Housing") of residents presents one of the major challenges every city must face. Adequate housing entails not only physical [shelters](/wiki/Shelter_(building) "Shelter (building)") but also the physical systems necessary to sustain life and economic activity.[[234]](#cite_note-236)

[Homeownership](/wiki/Owner-occupancy "Owner-occupancy") represents status and a modicum of economic security, compared to [renting](/wiki/Renting "Renting") which may consume much of the income of low-wage urban workers. [Homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness"), or lack of housing, is a challenge currently faced by millions of people in countries rich and poor.[[235]](#cite_note-237) Because cities generally have higher population densities than rural areas, city dwellers are more likely to reside in [apartments](/wiki/Apartment "Apartment") and less likely to live in a [single-family home](/wiki/Single-family_detached_home "Single-family detached home").

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Ecology")]

Main article: [Urban ecology](/wiki/Urban_ecology "Urban ecology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Trash_in_Paramaribo.JPG/250px-Trash_in_Paramaribo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Trash_in_Paramaribo.JPG)

An urban scene in [Paramaribo](/wiki/Paramaribo "Paramaribo") featuring a few plants growing amidst [solid waste](/wiki/Municipal_solid_waste "Municipal solid waste") and [rubble](/wiki/Rubble "Rubble") behind some houses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Urban_heat_island_%28Celsius%29.png/250px-Urban_heat_island_%28Celsius%29.png)](/wiki/File:Urban_heat_island_(Celsius).png)

An [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Dublin_Stephen%27s_Green-44_edit.jpg/250px-Dublin_Stephen%27s_Green-44_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dublin_Stephen%27s_Green-44_edit.jpg)

[St Stephen's Green](/wiki/St_Stephen%27s_Green "St Stephen's Green"), an [urban park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland

Urban [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"), influenced as they are by the density of human buildings and activities, differ considerably from those of their rural surroundings. Anthropogenic [buildings](/wiki/Building "Building") and [waste](/wiki/Waste "Waste"), as well as [cultivation](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") in [gardens](/wiki/Garden "Garden"), create physical and chemical environments which have no equivalents in the [wilderness](/wiki/Wilderness "Wilderness"), in some cases enabling exceptional [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"). They provide homes not only for immigrant humans but also for [immigrant plants](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species"), bringing about interactions between species that never previously encountered each other. They introduce frequent [disturbances](/wiki/Disturbance_(ecology) "Disturbance (ecology)") (construction, walking) to plant and animal [habitats](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat"), creating opportunities for [recolonization](/wiki/Secondary_succession "Secondary succession") and thus favoring [young ecosystems](/wiki/Ecological_succession "Ecological succession") with [r-selected species](/wiki/R/K_selection_theory "R/K selection theory") dominant. On the whole, urban ecosystems are less complex and productive than others, due to the diminished absolute amount of biological interactions.[[236]](#cite_note-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239)[[238]](#cite_note-PickettEtAl2008-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241)

Typical urban [fauna](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna") includes [insects](/wiki/Insect "Insect") (especially [ants](/wiki/Ant "Ant")), [rodents](/wiki/Rodent "Rodent") ([mice](/wiki/Mouse "Mouse"), [rats](/wiki/Rat "Rat")), and [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"), as well as [cats](/wiki/Cat "Cat") and [dogs](/wiki/Dog "Dog") ([domesticated](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication") and [feral](/wiki/Feral "Feral")). Large [predators](/wiki/Predator "Predator") are scarce.[[238]](#cite_note-PickettEtAl2008-240) However, in North America, large predators such as coyotes and other large animals like white-tailed deer persist.[[240]](#cite_note-242)

Cities generate considerable [ecological footprints](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint"), locally and at longer distances, due to concentrated populations and technological activities. From one perspective, cities are not ecologically [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") due to their resource needs. From another, proper management may be able to ameliorate a city's ill effects.[[241]](#cite_note-243)[[242]](#cite_note-244) [Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") arises from various forms of combustion,[[243]](#cite_note-245) including fireplaces, wood or coal-burning stoves, other heating systems,[[244]](#cite_note-246) and [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"). Industrialized cities, and today third-world megacities, are notorious for veils of [smog](/wiki/Smog "Smog") (industrial [haze](/wiki/Haze "Haze")) that envelop them, posing a chronic threat to the health of their millions of inhabitants.[[245]](#cite_note-Adey2013-247) Urban soil contains higher concentrations of [heavy metals](/wiki/Heavy_metals "Heavy metals") (especially [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), and [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel")) and has lower [pH](/wiki/PH "PH") than soil in the comparable wilderness.[[238]](#cite_note-PickettEtAl2008-240)

Modern cities are known for creating their own [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate"), due to [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete"), [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete"), and other artificial surfaces, which heat up in [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") and channel [rainwater](/wiki/Rain "Rain") into [underground ducts](/wiki/Storm_drain "Storm drain"). The [temperature in New York City](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_City#Climate "Geography of New York City") exceeds [nearby rural temperatures](/wiki/Climate_of_New_York_(state) "Climate of New York (state)") by an average of 2–3 °C and at times 5–10 °C differences have been recorded. This effect varies nonlinearly with population changes (independently of the city's physical size).[[238]](#cite_note-PickettEtAl2008-240)[[246]](#cite_note-248) Aerial [particulates](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates") increase rainfall by 5–10%. Thus, urban areas experience unique climates, with earlier flowering and later leaf dropping than in nearby countries.[[238]](#cite_note-PickettEtAl2008-240)

Poor and working-class people face disproportionate exposure to environmental risks (known as [environmental racism](/wiki/Environmental_racism "Environmental racism") when intersecting also with racial segregation). For example, within the urban microclimate, less-vegetated poor neighborhoods bear more of the heat (but have fewer means of coping with it).[[247]](#cite_note-249)

One of the main methods of improving the [urban ecology](/wiki/Urban_ecology "Urban ecology") is including in the cities more [urban green spaces](/wiki/Urban_green_space "Urban green space"): parks, gardens, lawns, and trees. These areas improve the health and well-being of the human, animal, and plant populations of the cities.[[248]](#cite_note-fuller-250) Well-maintained urban trees can provide many social, ecological, and physical benefits to the residents of the city.[[249]](#cite_note-251)

A study published in *[Scientific Reports](/wiki/Scientific_Reports "Scientific Reports")* in 2019 found that people who spent at least two hours per week in nature were 23 percent more likely to be satisfied with their life and were 59 percent more likely to be in good health than those who had zero exposure. The study used data from almost 20,000 people in the UK. Benefits increased for up to 300 minutes of exposure. The benefits are applied to men and women of all ages, as well as across different ethnicities, socioeconomic statuses, and even those with long-term illnesses and disabilities. People who did not get at least two hours – even if they surpassed an hour per week – did not get the benefits. The study is the latest addition to a compelling body of evidence for the health benefits of nature. Many doctors already give nature prescriptions to their patients. The study did not count time spent in a person's own yard or garden as time in nature, but the majority of nature visits in the study took place within two miles of home. "Even visiting local urban green spaces seems to be a good thing," Dr. White said in a press release. "Two hours a week is hopefully a realistic target for many people, especially given that it can be spread over an entire week to get the benefit."[[250]](#cite_note-252)

## World city system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: World city system")]

As the world becomes more closely linked through economics, politics, technology, and culture (a process called [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization")), cities have come to play a leading role in transnational affairs, exceeding the limitations of [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") conducted by national governments.[[251]](#cite_note-253)[[252]](#cite_note-HN2017-254)[[253]](#cite_note-Gupta2015globalization-255) This phenomenon, resurgent today, can be traced back to the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), [Phoenicia](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia"), and the Greek city-states, through the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") and other alliances of cities.[[254]](#cite_note-256)[[255]](#cite_note-CalderFreytas2009-257)[[256]](#cite_note-258) Today the [information economy](/wiki/Information_economy "Information economy") based on high-speed [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") infrastructure enables instantaneous [telecommunication](/wiki/Telecommunication "Telecommunication") around the world, effectively eliminating the distance between cities for the purposes of the international markets and other high-level elements of the world economy, as well as personal communications and [mass media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media").[[257]](#cite_note-259)

### Global city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Global city")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/London_Stock_Exchange_%2813056321704%29.jpg/250px-London_Stock_Exchange_%2813056321704%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_Stock_Exchange_(13056321704).jpg)

[Stock exchanges](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange"), characteristic features of the top global cities, are interconnected hubs for capital. Here, a delegation from Australia visits the [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange").

A [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city"), also known as a world city, is a prominent centre of trade, banking, finance, innovation, and markets.[[258]](#cite_note-260)[[259]](#cite_note-261) [Saskia Sassen](/wiki/Saskia_Sassen "Saskia Sassen") used the term "global city" in her 1991 work, *The Global City: New York, London, Tokyo* to refer to a city's [power](/wiki/Power_(social_and_political) "Power (social and political)"), status, and cosmopolitanism, rather than to its size.[[260]](#cite_note-262) Following this view of cities, it is possible to [rank the world's cities hierarchically](/wiki/Global_city#GaWC_study "Global city").[[261]](#cite_note-ranking-263) Global cities form the capstone of the global hierarchy, exerting [command and control](/wiki/Command_and_control "Command and control") through their economic and political influence. Global cities may have reached their status due to early transition to [post-industrialism](/wiki/Post-industrial_society "Post-industrial society")[[262]](#cite_note-264) or through inertia which has enabled them to maintain their dominance from the industrial era.[[263]](#cite_note-265) This type of ranking exemplifies an emerging [discourse](/wiki/Discourse "Discourse") in which cities, considered variations on the same ideal type, *must* compete with each other globally to achieve prosperity.[[182]](#cite_note-Ward2008-183)[[175]](#cite_note-Wachsmuth2014-176)

Critics of the notion point to the different realms of power and interchange. The term "global city" is heavily influenced by economic factors and, thus, may not account for places that are otherwise significant. [Paul James](/wiki/Paul_James_(academic) "Paul James (academic)"), for example argues that the term is "reductive and skewed" in its focus on financial systems.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

[Multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") and [banks](/wiki/Bank "Bank") make their headquarters in global cities and conduct much of their business within this context.[[265]](#cite_note-267) American firms dominate the international markets for [law](/wiki/Law_firm "Law firm") and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") and maintain branches in the biggest foreign global cities.[[266]](#cite_note-268)

Large cities have a great divide between populations of both ends of the financial spectrum.[[267]](#cite_note-269) Regulations on immigration promote the exploitation of low- and high-skilled immigrant workers from poor areas.[[268]](#cite_note-270)[[269]](#cite_note-271)[[270]](#cite_note-272) During employment, migrant workers may be subject to unfair working conditions, including working overtime, low wages, and lack of safety in workplaces.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200949–50-273)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/10_mile_panorama_of_NYC%2C_Feb.%2C_2018.jpg/1920px-10_mile_panorama_of_NYC%2C_Feb.%2C_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_mile_panorama_of_NYC,_Feb.,_2018.jpg "Modern global cities, like New York City, often include large central business districts (CBDs) that serve as hubs for economic activity. ### Transnational activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Transnational activity")]

Cities increasingly participate in world political activities independently of their enclosing nation-states. Early examples of this phenomenon are the [sister city](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") relationship and the promotion of [multi-level governance](/wiki/Multi-level_governance "Multi-level governance") within the European Union as a technique for [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration").[[252]](#cite_note-HN2017-254)[[272]](#cite_note-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275) Cities including [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), and [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") maintain their own embassies to [the European Union at Brussels](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union "Brussels and the European Union").[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278)

New urban dwellers are increasingly [transmigrants](/wiki/Transmigrants "Transmigrants"), keeping one foot each (through telecommunications if not travel) in their old and their new homes.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill200945–47-279)

### Global governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Global governance")]

Cities participate in [global governance](/wiki/Global_governance "Global governance") by various means including membership in global networks which transmit norms and regulations. At the general, global level, [United Cities and Local Governments](/wiki/United_Cities_and_Local_Governments "United Cities and Local Governments") (UCLG) is a significant [umbrella organization](/wiki/Umbrella_organization "Umbrella organization") for cities; regionally and nationally, [Eurocities](/wiki/Eurocities "Eurocities"), [Asian Network of Major Cities 21](/wiki/Asian_Network_of_Major_Cities_21 "Asian Network of Major Cities 21"), the [Federation of Canadian Municipalities](/wiki/Federation_of_Canadian_Municipalities "Federation of Canadian Municipalities") the [National League of Cities](/wiki/National_League_of_Cities "National League of Cities"), and the [United States Conference of Mayors](/wiki/United_States_Conference_of_Mayors "United States Conference of Mayors") play similar roles.[[278]](#cite_note-Bouteligier2013-280)[[279]](#cite_note-HN2017p82-281) UCLG took responsibility for creating [Agenda 21 for culture](/wiki/Agenda_21_for_culture "Agenda 21 for culture"), a program for [cultural policies](/wiki/Cultural_policy "Cultural policy") promoting sustainable development, and has organized various conferences and reports for its furtherance.[[280]](#cite_note-DuxburyJeannotte2013-282)

Networks have become especially prevalent in the arena of [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism") and specifically [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") following the adoption of [Agenda 21](/wiki/Agenda_21 "Agenda 21"). Environmental city networks include the [C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group](/wiki/C40_Cities_Climate_Leadership_Group "C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group"), the [United Nations Global Compact Cities Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Global_Compact#UN_Global_Compact_–_Cities_Programme "United Nations Global Compact"), the [Carbon Neutral Cities Alliance](/wiki/Carbon_Neutral_Cities_Alliance "Carbon Neutral Cities Alliance") (CNCA), the [Covenant of Mayors](/wiki/Covenant_of_Mayors "Covenant of Mayors") and the [Compact of Mayors](/wiki/Compact_of_Mayors "Compact of Mayors"),[[281]](#cite_note-283) [ICLEI – Local Governments for Sustainability](/wiki/ICLEI "ICLEI"), and the [Transition Towns network](/wiki/Transition_town "Transition town").[[278]](#cite_note-Bouteligier2013-280)[[279]](#cite_note-HN2017p82-281)

Cities hold world political status as meeting places for advocacy groups, non-governmental organizations, lobbyists, educational institutions, intelligence agencies, military contractors, information technology firms, and other groups with a stake in world policymaking. They are consequently also sites for symbolic protest.[[255]](#cite_note-CalderFreytas2009-257)[[c]](#cite_note-284)

[South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") has one of the highest rates of protests in the world.[[282]](#cite_note-285)

### United Nations System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: United Nations System")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/World_Bank_building_at_Washington.jpg/250px-World_Bank_building_at_Washington.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_Bank_building_at_Washington.jpg)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") headquarters in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/X_World_Assembly_of_Mayors_-_Quito_%2801%29.jpg/250px-X_World_Assembly_of_Mayors_-_Quito_%2801%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:X_World_Assembly_of_Mayors_-_Quito_(01).jpg)

The World Assembly of Mayors at the [Habitat III](/wiki/Habitat_III "Habitat III") conference in [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito")

The [United Nations System](/wiki/United_Nations_System "United Nations System") has been involved in a series of events and declarations dealing with the development of cities during this period of rapid urbanization.

* The [Habitat I](/wiki/Habitat_I "Habitat I") conference in 1976 adopted the "Vancouver Declaration on Human Settlements" which identifies urban management as a fundamental aspect of [development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development") and establishes various principles for maintaining urban [habitats](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat").[[283]](#cite_note-286)
* Citing the Vancouver Declaration, the UN General Assembly in December 1977 authorized the United Nations Commission Human Settlements and the HABITAT Centre for Human Settlements, intended to coordinate UN activities related to housing and settlements.[[284]](#cite_note-Walker2005-287)
* The 1992 [Earth Summit](/wiki/Earth_Summit "Earth Summit") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") resulted in a set of international agreements including [Agenda 21](/wiki/Agenda_21 "Agenda 21") which establishes principles and plans for [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development").[[285]](#cite_note-288)
* The [Habitat II](/wiki/Habitat_II "Habitat II") conference in 1996 called for cities to play a leading role in this program, which subsequently advanced the [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals") and [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals").[[286]](#cite_note-Parnell-289)
* In January 2002 the UN Commission on Human Settlements became an umbrella agency called the [United Nations Human Settlements Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Settlements_Programme "United Nations Human Settlements Programme") or UN-Habitat, a member of the [United Nations Development Group](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Group "United Nations Development Group").[[284]](#cite_note-Walker2005-287)
* The [Habitat III](/wiki/Habitat_III "Habitat III") conference of 2016 focused on implementing these goals under the banner of a "New Urban Agenda". The four mechanisms envisioned for effecting the New Urban Agenda are (1) national policies promoting integrated sustainable development, (2) stronger urban governance, (3) long-term integrated urban and territorial planning, and (4) effective financing frameworks.[[287]](#cite_note-Watson2016-290)[[288]](#cite_note-291) Just before this conference, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") concurrently approved an "Urban Agenda for the European Union" known as the [Pact of Amsterdam](/wiki/Pact_of_Amsterdam "Pact of Amsterdam").[[287]](#cite_note-Watson2016-290)

UN-Habitat coordinates the U.N. urban agenda, working with the [UN Environmental Programme](/wiki/UN_Environmental_Programme "UN Environmental Programme"), the [UN Development Programme](/wiki/UN_Development_Programme "UN Development Programme"), the [Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights](/wiki/Office_of_the_High_Commissioner_for_Human_Rights "Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights"), the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"), and the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[284]](#cite_note-Walker2005-287)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), a U.N. [specialized agency](/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations"), has been a primary force in promoting the Habitat conferences, and since the first Habitat conference has used their declarations as a framework for issuing loans for urban infrastructure.[[286]](#cite_note-Parnell-289) The bank's [structural adjustment](/wiki/Structural_adjustment "Structural adjustment") programs contributed to urbanization in the [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World") by creating incentives to move to cities.[[289]](#cite_note-292)[[290]](#cite_note-293) The World Bank and UN-Habitat in 1999 jointly established the [Cities Alliance](/wiki/Cities_Alliance "Cities Alliance") (based at the World Bank headquarters in Washington, D.C.) to guide policymaking, knowledge sharing, and [grant](/wiki/Grant_(money) "Grant (money)") distribution around the issue of urban poverty.[[291]](#cite_note-294) (UN-Habitat plays an advisory role in evaluating the quality of a locality's governance.)[[146]](#cite_note-Gupta2015p33-147) The Bank's policies have tended to focus on bolstering [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") markets through credit and technical assistance.[[292]](#cite_note-295)

The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization ([UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")) has increasingly focused on cities as key sites for influencing [cultural governance](/wiki/Cultural_governance "Cultural governance"). It has developed various city networks including the International Coalition of Cities against Racism and the Creative Cities Network. UNESCO's capacity to select [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") gives the organization significant influence over [cultural capital](/wiki/Cultural_capital "Cultural capital"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), and [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") funding.[[280]](#cite_note-DuxburyJeannotte2013-282)

## Representation in culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Representation in culture")]

Further information: [Urban fiction](/wiki/Urban_fiction "Urban fiction")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/The_fall_of_Babylon%3B_Cyrus_the_Great_defeating_the_Chaldean_Wellcome_V0034440.jpg/250px-The_fall_of_Babylon%3B_Cyrus_the_Great_defeating_the_Chaldean_Wellcome_V0034440.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_fall_of_Babylon;_Cyrus_the_Great_defeating_the_Chaldean_Wellcome_V0034440.jpg)

*The Fall of Babylon*, an 1831 portrait by [John Martin](/wiki/John_Martin_(painter) "John Martin (painter)"), depicts chaos with the Persian army occupying [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), symbolizing the ruin of a decadent civilization. The lightning striking the [Babylonian ziggurat](/wiki/Etemenanki "Etemenanki") represents the [Tower of Babel](/wiki/Tower_of_Babel "Tower of Babel") and God's judgment against Babylon.

Cities figure prominently in traditional Western culture, appearing in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") in both evil and holy forms, symbolized by [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").[[293]](#cite_note-296) [Cain](/wiki/Cain "Cain") and [Nimrod](/wiki/Nimrod "Nimrod") are the first city builders in the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis"). In Sumerian mythology [Gilgamesh](/wiki/Gilgamesh "Gilgamesh") built the walls of [Uruk](/wiki/Uruk "Uruk").

Cities can be perceived in terms of extremes or opposites: at once liberating and oppressive, wealthy and poor, organized and chaotic.[[294]](#cite_note-297) The name [anti-urbanism](/wiki/Anti-urbanism "Anti-urbanism") refers to various types of ideological opposition to cities, whether because of their culture or their political relationship with [the country](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area"). Such opposition may result from identification of cities with oppression and the ruling [elite](/wiki/Elite "Elite").[[295]](#cite_note-298) This and other political ideologies strongly influence narratives and themes in [discourse](/wiki/Discourse "Discourse") about cities.[[14]](#cite_note-KAL-14) In turn, cities symbolize their home societies.[[296]](#cite_note-299)

Writers, painters, and filmmakers have produced innumerable works of art concerning the urban experience. Classical and medieval literature includes a genre of *[descriptiones](/wiki/List_of_literary_descriptions_of_cities_(before_1550) "List of literary descriptions of cities (before 1550)")* which treat of city features and history. Modern authors such as [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") and [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce") are famous for evocative descriptions of their home cities.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009115-300) [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang") conceived the idea for his influential 1927 film *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* while visiting [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") and marveling at the nighttime [neon lighting](/wiki/Neon_lighting "Neon lighting").[[298]](#cite_note-301) Other early cinematic representations of cities in the twentieth century generally depicted them as technologically efficient spaces with smoothly functioning systems of automobile transport. By the 1960s, however, [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion") began to appear in such films as *[The Fast Lady](/wiki/The_Fast_Lady "The Fast Lady")* (1962) and *[Playtime](/wiki/Playtime "Playtime")* (1967).[[226]](#cite_note-Borden-228)

Literature, film, and other forms of popular culture have supplied visions of future cities both [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") and [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia"). The prospect of expanding, communicating, and increasingly interdependent world cities has given rise to images such as [Nylonkong](/wiki/Nylonkong "Nylonkong") (New York, London, Hong Kong)[[299]](#cite_note-302) and visions of a single world-encompassing [ecumenopolis](/wiki/Ecumenopolis "Ecumenopolis").[[300]](#cite_note-303)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-117)** Intellectuals such as [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), [Patrick Geddes](/wiki/Patrick_Geddes "Patrick Geddes") and [Kingsley Davis](/wiki/Kingsley_Davis "Kingsley Davis") foretold the coming of a mostly urban world throughout the twentieth century.[[111]](#cite_note-BrennerSchmid2013-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) The United Nations has long anticipated a half-urban world, earlier predicting the year 2000 as the turning point[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) and in 2007 writing that it would occur in 2008.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Other researchers had also estimated that the halfway point was reached in 2007.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Although the trend is undeniable, the precision of this statistic is dubious, due to reliance on national censuses and to the ambiguities of defining an area as urban.[[111]](#cite_note-BrennerSchmid2013-111)[[9]](#cite_note-Hugo-9)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-218)** Water resources in rapidly urbanizing areas are not merely *privatized* as they are in western countries; since the systems do not exist to begin with, private contracts also entail water [industrialization](/wiki/Water_industry "Water industry") and [enclosure](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/enclosure "wikt:enclosure").[[126]](#cite_note-Bakker2003-127) Also, there is a countervailing trend: 100 cities have *re*-municipalized their water supply since the 1990s.[[216]](#cite_note-217)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-284)** One important global political city, described at one time as a [world capital](/wiki/Caput_Mundi "Caput Mundi"), is [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") and [its metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), including [Tysons](/wiki/Tysons,_Virginia "Tysons, Virginia") and [Reston](/wiki/Reston,_Virginia "Reston, Virginia") in the [Dulles Technology Corridor](/wiki/Dulles_Technology_Corridor "Dulles Technology Corridor") and the various federal agencies found along the [Baltimore–Washington Parkway](/wiki/Baltimore%E2%80%93Washington_Parkway "Baltimore–Washington Parkway")). Beyond the prominent institutions of U.S. government on the national mall, this area contains [177 embassies](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_Washington,_DC "List of diplomatic missions in Washington, DC"), [The Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon"), the [Central Intelligence Agency headquarters](/wiki/George_Bush_Center_for_Intelligence "George Bush Center for Intelligence"), the World Bank headquarters, myriad [think tanks](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") and [lobbying groups](/wiki/Lobbyist "Lobbyist"), and corporate headquarters for [Booz Allen Hamilton](/wiki/Booz_Allen_Hamilton "Booz Allen Hamilton"), [General Dynamics](/wiki/General_Dynamics "General Dynamics"), [Capital One](/wiki/Capital_One "Capital One"), [Verisign](/wiki/Verisign "Verisign"), [Mortgage Electronic Registration Systems](/wiki/Mortgage_Electronic_Registration_Systems "Mortgage Electronic Registration Systems"), and [Gannett Company](/wiki/Gannett_Company "Gannett Company").[[255]](#cite_note-CalderFreytas2009-257)

92. **[^](#cite_ref-92)** Curtis (2016), pp. 5–6. "In the modern international system, cities were subjugated and internalized by the state, and, with industrialization, became the great growth engines of national economies."
93. **[^](#cite_ref-Blomley2013_93-0)** [Nicholas Blomley](/wiki/Nicholas_Blomley "Nicholas Blomley"), "What Sort of a Legal Space is a City?" in Brighenti (2013), pp. 1–20. "Municipalities, within this frame, are understood as nested within the jurisdictional space of the provinces. Indeed, rather than freestanding legal sites, they are imagined as products (or 'creatures') of the provinces who may bring them into being or dissolve them as they choose. As with the provinces their powers are of a delegated form: they may only exercise jurisdiction over areas that have been expressly identified by enabling legislation. Municipal law may not conflict with provincial law, and may only be exercised within its defined territory. [...]   
     Yet we are [in] danger [of] missing the reach of municipal law: '[e]ven in highly constitutionalized regimes, it has remained possible for municipalities to micro-manage space, time, and activities through police regulations that infringe both on constitutional rights and private property in often extreme ways' (Vaverde 2009: 150). While liberalism fears the encroachments of the state, it seems less worried about those of the municipality. Thus if a national government proposed a statute forbidding public gatherings or sporting events, a revolution would occur. Yet municipalities routinely enact sweeping by-laws directed at open ended (and ill-defined) offences such as loitering and obstruction, requiring permits for protests or requiring residents and homeowners to remove snow from the city's sidewalks."

96. **[^](#cite_ref-96)** Steven High, *[Industrial Sunset: The Making of North America's Rust Belt, 1969–1984](https://archive.org/details/industrialsunset0000high/page/5)*; University of Toronto Press, 2003; [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-8020-8528-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-8020-8528-8 "Special:BookSources/0-8020-8528-8"). "It is now clear that the deindustrialization thesis is part myth and part fact. Robert Z. Lawrence, for example, uses aggregate economic data to show that manufacturing employment in the United States did not decline but actually increased from 16.8 million in 1960, to 20.1 million in 1973, and 20.3 million in 1980. However, manufacturing employment was in relative decline. Barry Bluestone noted that manufacturing represented a decreasing proportion of the U.S. labour force, from 26.2 per cent in 1973 to 22.1 per cent in 1980. Studies in Canada have likewise shown that manufacturing employment was only in relative decline during these years. Yet mills and factories did close, and towns and cities lost their industries. John Cumbler submitted that 'depressions do not manifest themselves only at moments of national economic collapse' such as in the 1930s, but 'also recur in scattered sites across the nation in regions, in industries, and in communities.'"

Those who came to live in them did so in order to participate and compete on any attainable level. "Although studies of the so-called urban informal economy have shown myriad secret liaisons with outsourced multinational production systems, the larger fact is that hundreds of millions of new urbanites must further subdivide the peripheral economic niches of personal service, casual labor, street vending, rag picking, begging, and crime.   
      This outcast proletariat—perhaps 1.5 billion people today, 2.5 billion by 2030—is the fastest-growing and most novel social class on the planet. By and large, the urban informal working class is not a labor reserve army in the nineteenth-century sense: a backlog of strikebreakers during booms; to be expelled during busts; then reabsorbed again in the next expansion. On the contrary, this is a mass of humanity structurally and biologically redundant to the global accumulation and the corporate matrix.  
      It is ontologically both similar and dissimilar to the historical agency described in the *Communist Manifesto*. Like the traditional working classes, it has radical chains in the sense of having little vested interest in the reproduction of private property. But it is not a socialized collectivity of labor and it lacks significant power to disrupt or seize the means of production. It does possess, however, yet unmeasured powers of subverting urban order."

      No other city marketing opportunity achieves this global exposure. At the same time, provided it is carefully managed at the local level, it also gives a tremendous opportunity to heighten and mobilize the commitment of citizens to their own city. The competitive nature of sport and its unrivalled capacity to be enjoyed as a mass cultural activity gives it many advantages from the marketing point of view (S.V. Ward, 1998, pp. 231–232). Connected networks of protected settlements are inserted as islands of government control into insurgent areas—either defensively to separate existing populations from insurgents or aggressively as a means of extending control over areas—as used by the British in South Africa (1899–1902) and Malaya (1950–3) and by the Americans in Cuba (1898) and Vietnam (1965–75). These were generally small settlements and intended as much for local security as offensive operations. / The planned settlement policy of the State of Israel, however, has been both more comprehensive and has longer-term objectives. [...] These settlements provide a source of armed manpower, a defence in depth of a vulnerable frontier area and islands of cultural and political control in the midst of a potentially hostile population, thus continuing a tradition of the use of such settlements as part of similar policies in that area which is over 2,000 years old."

214. **[^](#cite_ref-215)** [Latham et al. 2009](#CITEREFLathamMcCormackMcNamaraMcNeill2009), p. 75: "By the 1960s, however, this 'integrated ideal' was being challenged, public infrastructure entering into crisis. There is now a new orthodoxy in many branches of urban planning: 'The logic is now for planners to fight for the best possible networked infrastructures for their specialized district, in partnership with (often privatised and internationalised network) operators, rather than seeking to orchestrate how networks roll out through the city as a whole" (Graham and Marvin, 2001: 113).  
     In the context of development theory, these 'secessionary' infrastructures physically by-pass sectors of cities unable to afford the necessary cabling, pipe-laying, or streetscaping that underpins service provision.
